P2.14.descope_recommendation:
Ship in two commits, not one — the two halves are independently valuable and independently testable:

v0 (M, ship first — 'selective offsite'): (1) Settings.offsite_events_only bool gating pending_offsite() via the eventless_segments-style EXISTS join; (2) the flagged_segment_paths() exemption threaded into recorder::prune() so a bookmarked event's segment can never be silently deleted by ordinary age/byte-cap retention (this is a real pre-existing gap and a prerequisite for 'back up bookmarked segments' to mean anything — without it, offsite selective backup can race retention and lose the very footage it's supposed to protect). Skip the hi/lo quality re-encode-before-upload entirely in v0 — always upload full quality when a segment is eligible. This alone is fully live-validatable and closes the most user-visible gap ('my bookmarked clip survived retention AND made it offsite').

v1 (deferred): (a) offsite_quality hi/lo — needs the new non-destructive recorder::reencode_to_temp() plus threading ffmpeg_bin through offsite.rs/lib.rs, and a policy decision on whether ffmpeg re-encoding inside the offsite worker's synchronous tick loop should lower MAX_PER_TICK (a 720p CRF30 re-encode of a 60s segment is not free — bounding matters so the offsite thread can't starve other workers); (b) true capacity-adaptive triggering of enhanced retention (today's enhanced_retention_days age-gate stays as the only trigger in v0 — making it ALSO fire on disk-pressure is a genuinely separate change to record.rs's retention-tick logic and its own live-validation story, distinct from the offsite-selection piece).

Rationale for not doing it all at once: the four sub-changes (query filter, retention exemption, non-destructive re-encode, capacity-trigger) touch three different subsystems (offsite worker, recorder crate's prune/reencode primitives, record.rs's retention tick) with three different failure modes to live-validate independently; bundling them risks a single validation pass missing a regression in one while confirming another.
open_questions: Should offsite_events_only / offsite_quality join the Admin-only settings gate (api.rs put_settings 5404-5426) alongside the destination/credential offsite_* fields, or is Operator-level fine since they only narrow (never widen) what's shipped versus today's ship-everything default? Leaning Operator-fine but worth a deliberate call.

Segments have no stored end_ts/duration (db.rs segments table, 1366-1372) — the events-overlap join has to approximate segment span via the GLOBAL settings.segment_seconds, which is wrong for any camera whose recording was interrupted mid-segment (shorter actual segment) or for a segment written under an old segment_seconds value before a settings change. eventless_segments() already accepts this approximation (with a margin param) — same tradeoff applies here; acceptable but worth calling out as a known imprecision, not a bug to chase.

For the byte-cap prune pass (record.rs 229-235, POOLED across all cameras), exempting flagged-event segments means the pool can legitimately exceed settings.retention_gb if enough footage is bookmarked — is that the desired product behavior (bookmarks are sacred, disk cap is best-effort) or should there be a secondary hard cap / user-facing warning when exempted bytes alone approach the configured GB? Recommend surfacing this in the Recordings storage callout (existing days_until_full/capacity UI in api.rs stats() ~4479-4523) as a follow-up, not blocking v0.

Does 'choose hi/lo stream' in the feature ask mean re-encode-before-upload (my read, reusing recorder::reencode_segment's engine) or does it mean choosing between an already-existing HIGH-res recording stream vs a separate LOW-res sub-stream recording (there is no such second recorded stream today — DetectConfig's sub-stream role, per CLAUDE.md, is used for detection sampling, not a second recorder/Recording instance) — worth confirming with the requester before building, since the sub-stream interpretation would be a much larger (new-recorder-per-camera) change than the re-encode interpretation.
P2.14.security_rbac_notes: Existing offsite_* Settings fields are gated Admin-only in put_settings because they control the exfiltration destination/credentials (api.rs 5404-5426, comment at 5399-5403 spells out the threat model: 'repointing them exfiltrates all recordings'). The two NEW fields (offsite_events_only, offsite_quality) don't touch destination/credentials and only ever REDUCE what leaves the box relative to today's default (back up everything) — so leaving them Operator-editable doesn't introduce a new escalation path. GET /api/offsite/status is unauthenticated-role-wise readable by any session (offsite_status handler has no explicit role check beyond the global auth middleware) and already avoids leaking secrets (offsite_secret_key never in the response) — if the two new fields are surfaced there, they're non-sensitive (booleans/enum), no new leak. The flagged_segment_paths() exemption in retention has no RBAC implication (it's a background worker, not an API surface) but DOES change disk-usage behavior in a way an Admin should be able to see (recommend it show up in the existing Recordings storage capacity callout, not a new endpoint).
shared_conflict_files: api.rs (put_settings validation block ~5394-5478, offsite_status handler ~1729-1758 — HIGH-CONTENTION file per the brief)
P2.14.summary: P2.14 asks for two related but distinct changes, both of which reuse heavily-already-shipped machinery rather than needing new subsystems. (1) Selective offsite: today offsite.rs's pending_offsite() (db.rs 3579-3602) backs up EVERY sealed segment unconditionally — needs a new Settings.offsite_events_only bool gating the query with an events-overlap EXISTS join, built by directly mirroring the exact NOT-EXISTS/BETWEEN-margin idiom eventless_segments() (db.rs 2329-2352) already uses for the symmetric 'no events' case. Critically, segments have no protection today from ordinary age/byte-cap retention deletion even when they underlie a flagged (bookmarked) event — only the EVENT ROW and its snapshot are protected (prune_events_before, db.rs 3793-3806); the raw recording segment can still be deleted out from under a bookmark by recorder::prune() (recorder/src/lib.rs 251-292, called from record.rs 224/231). Closing that gap (a new flagged_segment_paths() query threaded into prune() as an exempt-set) is a prerequisite for 'back up bookmarked segments' to be trustworthy, not an optional nice-to-have. (2) Capacity-adaptive quality degradation: this is ALREADY SHIPPED as 'enhanced retention' (record.rs 137-175, db.rs reduction_candidates/mark_segment_reduced, recorder::reencode_segment at recorder/src/lib.rs 224-247, all matrix #20) — it re-encodes old segments to 720p/CRF30 in place before the byte-cap prune can delete them outright. The only real gap versus the feature ask is that it's triggered PURELY by settings.enhanced_retention_days (age), never by actual disk-capacity pressure — making it also fire when nearing settings.retention_gb is a small, well-scoped extension of an existing tick loop. The 'choose hi/lo stream' half of the ask (upload a re-encoded low-bitrate copy instead of the original, without touching the on-disk recording) needs a genuinely new non-destructive variant of the existing reencode engine (recorder crate's private run_reencode/codec_args, 200-222, currently only exposed via the destructive reencode_segment) plus threading ffmpeg_bin into offsite.rs/lib.rs for the first time (offsite.rs has zero ffmpeg dependency today). Given four somewhat-independent sub-changes across three subsystems (offsite worker, recorder crate primitives, record.rs retention tick, plus the Settings/API/UI plumbing), this is realistically XL; the honest v0 slice is 'selective events-only backup + flagged-segment retention protection' (M), deferring hi/lo re-encoded upload and true capacity-triggered degradation to v1.
P2.14.validation_plan:
Against the running release NVR on :8080 (7 cameras, 5 enabled: 3,4,5,6,8):

1. Selective offsite (events_only): enable Settings > Offsite backup with a local MinIO/throwaway bucket already validated by the shipped feature (per changelog, the round-2 security suite live-validated bidirectional zip compat and the offsite worker against a real MinIO instance in an earlier session — reuse that setup). Toggle the new 'events only' checkbox on. Watch GET /api/offsite/status backlog/done counts and confirm via S3 bucket listing (or MinIO console) that ONLY keys corresponding to segments with a covering event appear — cross-check object_key()'s camera/date path against a known event's ts on one of the 5 enabled cameras. Toggle back off and confirm previously-skipped (no-event) segments start uploading on the next 30s tick.

2. Flagged-segment retention protection: on a camera with a short retention_days override (e.g. set camera 3's detect_config.retention_days=1 via Cameras tuning modal), bookmark (star) an event whose covering segment would otherwise age out within the test window; force retention.rs's RETENTION_EVERY tick (60s) to run past that segment's age cutoff; confirm via `GET /api/recordings` or direct segments table inspection that the bookmarked segment's file still exists on disk and its offsite_uploads row (if events_only+backup enabled) reaches 'done' — versus an equally-old UN-flagged segment on the same camera, which should be deleted as before.

3. (v1, if built) hi/lo quality: set offsite_quality='lo', confirm uploaded object bytes are smaller than the local segment's bytes (compare S3 HEAD content-length vs local segments.bytes) and that the LOCAL file is untouched (same size/mtime before and after an upload tick) — this is the critical regression check since the reused reencode engine is destructive by default (reencode_segment renames over the original) and must NOT do that here.

4. (v1, if built) capacity-adaptive trigger: temporarily lower settings.retention_gb near current total_bytes (from GET /api/stats), confirm the enhanced-retention block fires reduction_candidates()/reencode_segment() on event-bearing segments even with enhanced_retention_days=0, and that GET /api/stats total_bytes drops accordingly on the next tick.

All Rust changes: cargo clippy -D warnings + cargo test (recorder crate's prune tests updated for the new exempt-set param; db.rs gets a unit test for the new events-overlap filter mirroring the existing eventless_segments_query test at db.rs:4823) before any live restart; release rebuild + detached Start-Process restart per the documented GOTCHA (never a timed background shell — the data-dir lock will correctly refuse a double-start).

P3.8.descope_recommendation: v0 (recommended, effort M): continuous physical recording is architecturally required for real pre-roll — ffmpeg's `-f segment` writer (recorder::Recording::start, recorder/src/lib.rs:58) only produces files at 60s segment boundaries and there is no packet ring buffer anywhere in the stack, so 'gating the recorder' cannot mean literally starting/stopping ffmpeg per-trigger without sacrificing pre-roll entirely. The correct, honest v0 is: keep the continuous segmenter running unchanged (zero changes to recorder crate or the reconcile/start-stop logic at record.rs:56-115), and implement 'record only around detections' as a NEW, much tighter retention mode layered beside the existing event_only_recording — same NOT-EXISTS-nearby-event deletion primitive, but (a) a per-camera configurable pre/post-roll in seconds instead of the fixed segment-span margin, and (b) a short settle grace (post_roll+30s) instead of the existing flat 15-minute review grace, so quiet cameras shed disk almost as fast as events stop happening. This ships the full user-visible feature (huge storage win on low-activity cameras, configurable pre/post-roll, a first-class UI choice distinct from continuous+retention) with zero migrations, zero new endpoints, and no touch to pipeline.rs/lib.rs/api.ts/App.tsx/mqtt.rs/notify.rs. Explicitly defer: sub-segment trimming (only whole 60s segments are kept/dropped, so worst-case retained footage is up to ~1 segment-length more than the configured pre/post-roll — same granularity event_only_recording already accepts); a byte/GB estimate-savings preview in the UI; a global Settings-level default pre/post-roll (hardcode sane defaults — 10s/20s — instead, mirroring how min_score/motion_threshold have per-camera Option<> inherit but this is genuinely new config with no existing global counterpart to inherit from)."]

open_questions: Should `trigger_recording` and `event_only_recording` be truly mutually exclusive at the data layer (a single `record_mode: enum` replacing both booleans) or just mutually-exclusive-by-UI-convention (both booleans kept, UI just toggles the other off)? Recommend the latter for v0 — zero migration risk, both retention loops are independently idempotent (a segment already deleted by one loop is a harmless no-op DELETE for the other) if a config somehow has both flags set (e.g. via direct API/backup-restore).

Should pre/post-roll seconds be a per-camera-only knob (as scoped) or also get a global Settings.trigger_pre_roll_secs/post_roll_secs default that per-camera Option<> fields inherit (the 'using global: Xs' UX pattern used for retention_days/poll_ms)? Descoped for v0 — hardcoded defaults (10s/20s) keep this out of the high-contention Settings.tsx + Settings struct.

Longer post-roll review window risk: if a user views/exports a clip (event_clip / evidence / share) after `post_roll + 30s` has elapsed, the covering segment may already be pruned (extract_event_clip's find_segment_at returns None → 404 'no recording covers this event'). This is the SAME pre-existing tradeoff event_only_recording already has (15-min grace vs. an arbitrarily-delayed review) — just with a much shorter window by design (that's the point of the feature). Worth a one-line honest caveat in the Feature help text, consistent with CLAUDE.md's honesty-first UX bar.

Is a segment-count/estimated-storage-savings preview (like the Alarm rule-preview or clip-share UI patterns) worth adding in v0, or pure text copy ('keeps only ~pre+post seconds around each detection') is enough? Recommend text-only for v0 given the M-effort target.
P3.8.security_rbac_notes: No new endpoint and no new auth surface: the feature rides the existing `PATCH /api/cameras/{id}` route, which already RBAC-scopes writes through `require_camera(&allowed_cameras(&st, &p)?, id)` (api.rs:1478) — an Operator/Viewer without access to a camera cannot flip this flag on it, identical to every other DetectConfig field. No new file paths, no new SSRF/path-traversal surface (record.rs's new loop only ever calls `db.delete_segment_by_path` on paths that came out of the `segments` table itself, same as the existing event_only_recording loop). No credentials or PII involved. The only thing worth a light server-side clamp (not strictly required — retention_days/poll_ms set this precedent of clamping only at the consumption site, not in patch_camera) is bounding trigger_pre_roll_secs/trigger_post_roll_secs to sane ranges (e.g. 1..=120 / 1..=300) in record.rs so a garbage/huge value from a hand-crafted PATCH can't produce a pathological "never prune" retention window — this is proposed inline via `.clamp(...)` at the point of use, matching ClipQuery's existing clamp pattern (api.rs:2770-2771).
P3.8.summary: Detection-triggered recording is best implemented NOT as a literal start/stop-ffmpeg trigger (impossible to get real pre-roll from, since there's no packet ring buffer anywhere in the stack — recorder::Recording::start always segments continuously via `ffmpeg -f segment`), but as a tighter, configurable sibling of the already-shipped event-only retention (`DetectConfig.event_only_recording`, record.rs:177-205, backed by `db::eventless_segments`). Recording stays continuous (zero changes to the reconcile/start-stop logic in record.rs:56-115 or to the `recorder` crate) — real pre-roll footage physically exists only because the segmenter never stopped. The new mode instead prunes far more aggressively and asymmetrically than event-only recording's fixed 15-minute-grace/segment-span-margin: a new `trigger_recording` DetectConfig flag with configurable `trigger_pre_roll_secs`/`trigger_post_roll_secs` drives a parallel retention loop in record.rs that widens the existing `eventless_segments` query (db.rs:2329) to take separate before/after margins instead of one symmetric margin, and uses a short `post_roll+30s` settle grace instead of the hardcoded 15 minutes — so a quiet camera sheds disk almost as fast as its detections stop, while a segment overlapping any event's [ts-pre, ts+post] window survives. Everything rides the existing `detect_json` JSON-blob column (zero migration) and the existing `PATCH /api/cameras/{id}` route (zero new endpoint, RBAC inherited for free). Web side mirrors the exact `event_only_recording`/`retention_days` UI patterns already in Cameras.tsx's 'Recording & retention' section (tab==='stream'), upgraded to a 3-way `.arm-bar` segmented 'Recording mode' control (Continuous / Event-only / Detection-triggered) reusing the identical CSS Home.tsx already uses for its Security-mode selector — no new styles.css needed. Total surgical footprint: 1 signature change to `eventless_segments` (1 call site, 1 test), 1 new retention loop in record.rs, 3 new DetectConfig fields, and a Cameras.tsx UI section — pipeline.rs, lib.rs, App.tsx, mqtt.rs, notify.rs, styles.css are untouched.

P3.6.summary: pipeline::run (crates/core/src/pipeline.rs lines 117-1331) is one OS thread that serially iterates all enabled/detect cameras (`cameras.iter().filter(|c| c.enabled && c.detect)`, line 251), and for each camera does a BLOCKING `ureq::get(...).call()` frame fetch (fetch_frame, line 1992-2004, 5s timeout) before motion/YOLO/face/LPR/CLIP/gait/analytics. A single stalled camera stalls every camera for up to 5s per tick. The minimal correct worker pool: split the existing function into (1) `fn run_worker(worker_idx, num_workers, db, go2rtc, snapshots_dir, status, mqtt_tx, throttle, genai_tx, shutdown)` — today's body verbatim, except the camera filter at line 251 (and the two tick-level `.any(...)` gait/face-preload gates at lines 219-250) become position-based: `cameras.iter().enumerate().filter(|(i,c)| c.enabled && c.detect && i % num_workers == worker_idx)`. Because every per-camera cache in `run` (detectors, gates, trackers, analytics, residential, tamper_gates, gait_states, packages, alerted_tracks, last_event, motion_acc, ptz_capable, last_autotrack, last_unknown_save, face_engine, clip, lpr, prompt_embs — declared lines 129-177) is a local HashMap keyed by camera id, sharding by camera means each worker owns a fully disjoint slice of that state with zero new locking, and Detector (an ONNX Session) never has to cross a thread boundary — each worker builds/owns its own detector cache exactly like today, just on N threads instead of 1. (2) a new outer `pub fn run(...)` with the SAME external signature as today (so lib.rs's existing thread-spawn + join at lib.rs:183-193/408 needs zero changes) that reads `db.settings().detect_workers`, clamps it (defense against an unvalidated value arriving via POST /api/restore -> db.save_settings, which bypasses put_settings' bounds checks), spawns that many named `std::thread::Builder` workers cloning the already-Clone db/go2rtc/status/mqtt_tx/throttle/genai_tx/snapshots_dir/shutdown, and joins them. Plus one new Settings.detect_workers: u32 field (JSON blob, no migration), one put_settings bound, one api.ts field, one Settings.tsx input. Per-camera force_cpu (already exists) doubles as the \"bind to a different accelerator\" lever: a worker whose shard happens to contain only force_cpu cameras naturally runs a CPU-only detector cache concurrently with a GPU-only shard on another thread — no new pinning primitive required for v0.

files_to_edit.why: The whole feature lives here. Current `pub fn run(db, go2rtc, snapshots_dir, status, mqtt_tx, throttle, genai_tx, shutdown)` (lines 117-1331) is a single OS-thread loop that serially iterates `cameras.iter().filter(|c| c.enabled && c.detect)` (line 251) and for each camera does a BLOCKING `ureq::get(...).call()` HTTP fetch (fetch_frame, line 1992-2004, 5s timeout) + JPEG decode + motion gate + YOLO + face/LPR/CLIP/gait/analytics enrichment, all owning private per-tick HashMaps (detectors, gates, trackers, analytics, residential, tamper_gates, gait_states, packages, alerted_tracks, last_event, motion_acc, ptz_capable, last_autotrack, last_unknown_save, face_engine, clip, lpr, prompt_embs — all declared lines 129-177) that never cross a thread boundary today. Plan: (1) rename this function to `fn run_worker(worker_idx: usize, num_workers: usize, db: Db, go2rtc: Arc<Go2Rtc>, snapshots_dir: PathBuf, status: StatusBoard, mqtt_tx: Sender<mqtt::EventMsg>, throttle: AlarmThrottle, genai_tx: Sender<genai::Job>, shutdown: Arc<AtomicBool>)`, body unchanged except the camera filter at line 251 becomes `cameras.iter().enumerate().filter(|(i, c)| c.enabled && c.detect && i % num_workers == worker_idx).map(|(_, c)| c)` (position-based round-robin over the stable `ORDER BY id` list from db.list_cameras(), NOT `cam.id % num_workers` which can collide/imbalance on sparse ids) — and the same enumerate+modulo predicate needs threading into the two tick-level `cameras.iter().any(|c| ...)` gates that decide whether to bother loading `gait_profiles` (lines 219-234) and `enrolled_faces` (lines 240-250), so a worker whose shard has no gait/face cameras doesn't do that DB work every tick. (2) add a new `pub fn run(db, go2rtc, snapshots_dir, status, mqtt_tx, throttle, genai_tx, shutdown)` — SAME external signature as today, zero lib.rs changes required — that reads `db.settings().detect_workers` ONCE, clamps it `.clamp(1, 16)` defensively (Settings can arrive unvalidated via POST /api/restore -> db.save_settings, which bypasses put_settings's bounds checks — see api.rs:1645), spawns that many `std::thread::Builder::new().name(format!("detector-{i}"))` threads each cloning db/go2rtc/snapshots_dir/status/mqtt_tx/throttle/genai_tx/shutdown (all already Clone; mqtt_tx/genai_tx are mpsc::Sender, cloned exactly like lib.rs already does for mqtt_tx2/mqtt_tx_tr/etc.) and calling run_worker(i, num_workers, ...), then joins all handles before returning. This keeps pipeline::run blocking until shutdown exactly as before, so lib.rs's det_thread spawn (lib.rs:183-193) and its `det_thread.join()` (lib.rs:408) need NO changes.

P3.6.descope_recommendation: v0 (recommended): pure mechanical worker-pool split inside pipeline.rs — spawn N OS threads at pipeline::run startup, each running a full independent copy of today's per-camera loop (its own detector/gate/tracker/etc. caches) over a STATIC position-based shard of `cameras` (index % num_workers, recomputed fresh from the current camera list every tick, so add/remove/enable/disable cameras re-shard automatically — only the worker COUNT is fixed for the process lifetime). One new Settings.detect_workers: u32 (default 1, byte-identical behavior to today), one Settings.tsx field, one put_settings bound. This alone delivers the real, measurable win: today a single stalled/slow camera's 5s ureq fetch_frame timeout (pipeline.rs line 1995) blocks EVERY other camera's detection for that tick; with N>=2 workers a stalled camera only blocks its own shard. Explicitly deferred to v1: (a) an explicit per-camera `detect_config.worker_index` pin for deterministic "put these 2 GPU cameras on worker 0, these 3 CPU cameras on worker 1" placement (v0 gets a coarse version of this for free — a worker whose shard happens to contain only force_cpu=true cameras naturally builds a CPU-only detector cache — but it's not user-controllable); (b) hot-reloading detect_workers without a restart (today's `gkey`/`dkey` per-tick model-swap pattern doesn't extend cleanly to changing thread COUNT mid-run); (c) a `zoomy_detect_workers` Prometheus gauge; (d) dynamic load-based rebalancing instead of static position sharding.
open_questions: Does DirectML (Windows target OS in this environment) support multiple concurrent ONNX Runtime sessions from different OS threads hitting the same physical GPU without contention/errors? Each worker builds its own independent Detector/Session (no shared session, no locking), which is the safe design, but this has not been validated live with num_workers>1 against a real DirectML GPU — flag as the top live-validation risk.

Is index-based round-robin sharding (cameras.iter().enumerate(), i % num_workers) acceptable, or does the project want deterministic sticky assignment so a given camera always lands on the same worker across ticks even as OTHER cameras are added/removed/reordered (today's `ORDER BY id` list only reorders on insert/delete, so in practice a camera's shard is stable unless the camera set changes — likely fine, but worth confirming against the 'live-validation' 7-cam/5-enabled test fixture)?

Should detect_workers be capped lower than 16 given this is meant for a home NVR (test env has 5 enabled cameras) — 1..=8 might be a more honest upper bound to avoid a user thread-storming a small machine via a crafted /api/restore payload before the clamp() in pipeline.rs even runs (clamp happens at spawn time, so worst case is a brief burst of thread-Builder calls, not sustained, but still worth picking a sane number)?

Should there be a minimum-per-worker floor (e.g. warn/no-op if num_workers > enabled camera count, since an idle worker thread with zero shard cameras just spins on the tick-sleep loop doing nothing but wasted wakeups)?
P3.6.security_rbac_notes: Settings.detect_workers is a plain Operator-writable detection-tuning knob (same class as confidence/poll_ms/motion_threshold) — no new RBAC gate needed in put_settings' existing Admin-only allowlist (api.rs lines 5404-5426, which only covers auth_proxy_*/offsite_* fields). The one real hardening point: POST /api/restore -> `restore()` (api.rs:1635-1645) calls `st.db.save_settings(&b.settings)` DIRECTLY, bypassing put_settings' bounds validation entirely (this is a pre-existing pattern for every Settings field, not new to this feature — e.g. a malicious backup could already set poll_ms:0 or confidence:99). A crafted/corrupted backup JSON could set detect_workers to an absurd value (e.g. u32::MAX) and cause a thread-spawn resource-exhaustion DoS on the NEXT process start. Mitigate by clamping `num_workers = settings.detect_workers.clamp(1, N)` INSIDE pipeline::run itself (not only in put_settings), so the defense holds regardless of how Settings reached the DB — consistent with this codebase's established defense-in-depth style (e.g. status::freshness_window's saturating_mul guard against an absurd operator-set poll_ms).

P3.1.summary: Journey fusion is almost entirely a reuse story. The cross-camera appearance-similarity primitive is already shipped and RBAC-hardened: GET /api/events/{id}/similar (api.rs:3829 event_similar) cosine-ranks db.crop_embeddings() (db.rs:3221) against a seed event's crop embedding, camera-scoping every result, and the web client (api.ts:846 eventSimilar) + a full results grid already render in Events.tsx's 'Similar' modal (state at 230, fetch at 473-481, render 1398-1475). There is deliberately no cross-camera track linkage in crates/tracker (Track.history at tracker/src/lib.rs:107 is per-camera-instance only) — crop-embedding cosine search is correctly the only fusion primitive available, exactly matching the brief. So v0 of this feature is a pure web composition: add a Grid/Journey view toggle to the existing Similar modal; Journey mode re-sorts the same already-fetched results chronologically into a numbered narrative, lazily fetches Settings.floorplan (already Viewer-readable, FloorPlan.tsx's existing model) to draw a path across camera pins in a new small JourneyMap.tsx component (reusing .fp-wrap/.fp-pin CSS), and mounts the existing CrossTimeline.tsx component (unmodified) scoped to just the involved cameras for a synchronized mini-timeline, with clicks reusing the same setOpen(event) viewer-opening pattern the Grid view already uses. Zero new backend routes, zero DB changes, zero App.tsx/api.ts changes for v0 — every touchpoint is additive and self-contained in Events.tsx + one new component. The 'appearance-similarity, never identity certainty' framing rides the existing similarity % badges plus a persistent callout-info disclaimer, consistent with the codebase's established liability-framing pattern (residential/pose assistive disclaimers, TuneModal callouts). The optional stitched 'Moments' export clip is real new backend work (multi-camera, multi-resolution ffmpeg concat via filter_complex — nothing like it exists yet, since build_timelapse (api.rs:3139) only concats same-camera same-resolution segments via the concat demuxer) and is cleanly deferred to v1, reusing extract_event_clip (2761), run_ffmpeg_atomic (2717), and the drawtext-watermark escaping pattern from ensure_evidence_mp4 (3205-3260) plus the .building-marker background-job/poll idiom from create_timelapse (3008-3169).

P3.1.descope_recommendation: Ship v0 with ZERO backend/API changes: extend Events.tsx's existing Similar modal with a Journey view (chronological narrative + JourneyMap path + a scoped CrossTimeline), reusing GET /api/events/{id}/similar and GET /api/settings verbatim. Defer the 'optional stitched Moments export clip' (explicitly called optional in the brief) to v1 — it needs genuinely new Rust: a multi-input ffmpeg filter_complex concat with per-clip resolution normalization (scale+pad+setsar+concat), unlike anything in the codebase today (build_timelapse only concats same-camera segments via the simple concat demuxer). v0 has no shared-file contention at all and is safe to build in any wave; schedule v1 after other api.rs-touching work lands.

P3.2.summary: Ship a new synchronous, request/response 'Ask your cameras' capability: a bounded, READ-ONLY tool-calling loop that lets a user type a natural-language question ('how many people came to the front door today?') and get back a grounded answer plus cited events. Backend: a new crates/core/src/ask.rs module speaks the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions 'tools' protocol (NOT genai.rs's native Ollama /api/generate — that's vision-captioning only; ask.rs needs a distinct chat+tools shape) over ureq, reusing genai::call_vision's exact request pattern (POST, optional Bearer header, 60s timeout, JSON in/out) but with new body/response parsing for tool_calls. It exposes exactly 3 v0 tools backed by existing, already-RBAC-proven DB queries: list_cameras (db.list_cameras + StatusBoard), search_events (mirrors list_events's WHERE-clause shape at db.rs:1747-1789), and a new thin db::count_events (a SELECT COUNT(*) mirroring the same WHERE clause, since letting the model 'count' by eyeballing a capped result list is unreliable). Every tool call is executed server-side against the caller's allowed_cameras set (api.rs:1149-1186) BEFORE hitting the DB — the model's arguments are never trusted, only used to parameterize the same RBAC-scoped queries every other endpoint already uses. The loop is hard-capped (a handful of rounds) and runs inside tokio::task::spawn_blocking + an outer tokio::time::timeout, exactly like push_test/discover's blocking-ureq pattern. New Settings fields (ask_enabled/ask_url/ask_model/ask_api_key) ride the existing settings JSON blob (db.rs:1006-1206) — no migration. Off by default; the endpoint 400s until configured, mirroring genai_enabled's gate. Web: a new Ask.tsx page (single question box, answer, an expandable 'how I found this' tool trace, and cited-event thumbnails deep-linking to #/events/<id> via the already-shipped focusEvent mechanism) plus the usual App.tsx nav/lazy-route wiring and a Settings.tsx card cloned from the AI-captions card. This is docs/08 P3.2 / docs/06 row 6 verbatim — those docs already name the exact shape (bounded tool-loop over events/search/counts, needs a tool-calling model, treat tool output as untrusted, off by default) and even flag the key nuance: untrusted flows BOTH ways — the model's tool args are untrusted (validate/RBAC-clamp before querying) AND the DB rows fed back to the model are untrusted (transcripts/captions/notes are partly attacker-influenceable text that could contain a prompt-injection attempt) — the system prompt must say tool results are DATA, not instructions, and zero mutating tools are ever registered.

P3.2.descope_recommendation: v0: exactly 3 read-only tools (list_cameras, search_events, count_events) covering the prompt's own 'events/search/counts' framing; a single-turn Q&A UI (no persisted multi-turn chat, no streaming/SSE); a hard per-request tool-call round cap + outer wall-clock timeout; Admin-gated endpoint/key changes; one audit_log row per question. Defer to v1: a CLIP smart-search tool (needs models_present() gating, reuses smart_search's scoring logic at api.rs:3730-3823), an event_similar/appearance tool, persisted multi-turn conversation history (would need a new DB table — the only part of this feature that would actually need a migration), streaming responses, a dedicated per-user/IP rate limiter to bound BYO-cloud cost (no generic one exists today — LoginThrottle is login-specific), and a focused adversarial prompt-injection red-team pass (do a basic system-prompt hardening pass in v0, but a dedicated verify workflow is v1 per docs/06's explicit 'live-validate prompt-injection resistance' callout).
P3.2.security_rbac_notes: Two distinct untrusted-data directions, both must be handled: (1) the model's tool-call ARGUMENTS are untrusted — never trust an LLM-supplied camera_id/limit directly; every tool execution must intersect with allowed_cameras (api.rs:1149-1186) exactly like every other RBAC-scoped endpoint, and only the 3 whitelisted read-only tool names are ever dispatched (an unrecognized tool name from a hallucinating/malicious BYO model returns an error object to the model, not a panic or a fallback to arbitrary SQL). (2) the DB rows fed BACK to the model as tool results are untrusted text — user-writable event notes/tags and model-written transcripts/captions could carry a prompt-injection attempt; the system prompt must state tool output is DATA not instructions, and since there are zero mutating tools registered, the blast radius of a successful injection is capped at 'the model says something wrong in its answer text', not an actual action. Settings changes to ask_url/ask_enabled/ask_api_key should require Admin (like offsite_backup_enabled/offsite_endpoint) since repointing the endpoint silently exfiltrates future questions (which necessarily embed event metadata) to a new destination. GET /api/settings must never echo ask_api_key in the clear (mirror smtp_pass/offsite_secret_key's blank-on-read). The /api/ask endpoint itself should stay Operator+ (the existing POST default) rather than Viewer, since — unlike a plain GET search — it can incur real per-question cost against a paid BYO cloud endpoint.

P2.8b-feedback-learning.descope_recommendation:
Full-correctness v1 requires gating THREE alarm-firing paths in pipeline.rs, because the codebase currently computes an event's CLIP crop embedding in a SECOND pass (pipeline.rs:1250-1319, capped 6/frame) that runs AFTER the ordinary immediate label-match alarm rules already fired synchronously (pipeline.rs:1081-1129). Only the P2.2 prompt-rule path (`fire_prompt_alarms`, already gated at crop-embed time) and the VLM-deferred path (`genai::vlm_gate`, which runs seconds later on a worker thread, well after the crop embedding lands in the DB) get the crop embedding 'for free' with no timing conflict. Gating the plain/immediate label-match rules — the majority of real-world alarm rules — requires hoisting a bounded, capped crop-embed pass to run BEFORE line 1081 (mirroring the existing upfront face/plate precompute at pipeline.rs:906-997), which touches the same hot, adversarially-reviewed detection loop that already handles #61 Re-ID, P2.2 prompts, gait, and stationary suppression.

RECOMMENDED v0 (M effort): ship suppression ONLY where crop embeddings are already available with zero pipeline restructuring — `fire_prompt_alarms` (pipeline.rs:1851, add a `sup_embs` early-return at the top) and `genai::vlm_gate` (genai.rs:210, DB lookup before the `notify::fire` call at line 275). Document plainly (UI copy + code comment) that v0 feedback suppression applies to AI-verified/prompt-based rules, not yet plain label-match rules — this is honest, low-risk, and covers the noisiest/fuzziest alert types first (exactly where false positives are most likely). Ship the thumbs-down button, the new table, and the endpoint regardless — they're needed either way.

v1 (the extra L-effort slice): hoist a capped upfront crop-embed pass before pipeline.rs:999 (reusing `embed_crop`, lazy-initing `clip` earlier) so the immediate label-match loop (1081-1093) can also AND in `!alert_suppressed`, at the cost of a small amount of redundant CLIP inference vs. the existing loop-2 pass (or a follow-up cleanup to have loop 2 reuse the precomputed embedding instead of recomputing it).

P2.8b-feedback-learning.security_rbac_notes: Identical shape to the already-shipped `bookmark_event`/`event_similar`: the new endpoint loads the event first (`get_event`) then calls `require_camera(&allowed_cameras(&st,&p)?, ev.camera_id)` so a camera-scoped Operator/Viewer can only submit feedback for events on cameras they can already see (404, not 403, to avoid camera-id enumeration — matches the existing convention). No new route needs a auth.rs special-case: `min_role_for` already defaults every non-GET method to Operator (auth.rs:173-174), so Viewers are rejected automatically, same as bookmark_event today. The suppression corpus itself (raw float embeddings, keyed by camera_id+label) is never exposed via a read API in v0, so there's no cross-camera information leak; write-time camera scoping is the only enforcement needed. Every gate this feature adds must fail OPEN on any DB error or missing embedding (mirrors `AlarmRule::confirm_ok` at db.rs:786-799 and `vlm_gate`'s `None`-on-error handling at genai.rs:222-226) — an infra glitch or an event with no crop must never silently swallow a real alert. Nothing here changes what gets recorded to disk (events/snapshots still write normally) or what audit_log captures — only whether `notify::fire` is reached, so evidentiary integrity (#40/#52) is unaffected.
P2.8b-feedback-learning.summary: P2.8b hooks a fail-open, per-(camera,label) cosine-similarity gate onto three alarm-firing sites, all reusing the crop-embedding infrastructure #61 already shipped (`event_embeddings.crop_embedding`, `db.crop_embedding_for`, `smart::cosine`). The one real wrinkle the recon surfaced: the pipeline computes an event's crop embedding in a SECOND pass (pipeline.rs:1250-1319) that runs strictly AFTER the ordinary immediate label-match alarm rules already fired (pipeline.rs:1081-1129) — so those plain rules cannot be gated by a same-tick crop comparison without hoisting the embed earlier. Two paths already sidestep this: `fire_prompt_alarms` (P2.2, pipeline.rs:1851) fires exactly where crop_emb is produced, and `genai::vlm_gate` (deferred VLM-verified rules) runs seconds later on its own worker thread, well after the crop embedding has landed in the DB. The recommended v0 wires suppression into those two paths only (new `smart::any_similar` helper, a new bounded `alert_feedback` table storing camera_id+label+embedding mirroring `add_notification`'s self-trim, a `POST /api/events/{id}/feedback` endpoint cloned from `bookmark_event`'s RBAC shape, and a 'Not this' button next to the existing 'Similar' button in Events.tsx's lightbox — `IconThumbDown` is already defined in icons.tsx, unused). Full v1 coverage of plain label-match rules requires hoisting a capped crop-embed pass earlier in pipeline.rs's per-camera loop (reusing the existing `embed_crop`/`MAX_CROPS_PER_FRAME` machinery), which is a larger, riskier touch to an already-dense, adversarially-reviewed hot path — spelled out as the descope.
P2.8b-feedback-learning.validation_plan: 1. Build: cargo clippy -D warnings + cargo test (new db.rs `alert_feedback` round-trip test + smart.rs `any_similar` test) + web tsc/vite green. 2. Stop the running NVR, rebuild release, restart via detached `Start-Process` (per memory gotcha — never a timed background shell) against the real data dir with 7 cameras. 3. On a camera with recent object detections, open Events, open an event's lightbox, first confirm `/api/events/{id}/similar` returns `available:true` for it (proves a crop embedding exists), then click 'Not this' -> expect a success toast; verify a new row landed in `alert_feedback` (sqlite3 on data/zoomy.db) with the right camera_id/label. 4. Re-present a cosine-similar object to that camera (re-trigger the same physical object/vehicle, or replay the same synthetic clip) with a prompt-based (P2.2) or vlm_prompt rule armed on that camera+label: confirm the EVENT still gets created (`GET /api/events` lists it — suppression must only silence the alert, not the detection) but no webhook/ntfy fires and `tracing`/service.log shows the new 'feedback gate: suppressed' line instead of the usual fire log. 5. Confirm a DIFFERENT object/label on the same camera still fires normally (no over-suppression) — this also exercises the per-label scoping decision. 6. Fail-open check: thumbs-down an event with no crop embedding (e.g. a YAMNet audio event or a camera with CLIP models not installed) -> expect `{ok:false, reason:"no_crop"}` and confirm normal alerts on that camera/event type are completely unaffected by any feedback state. 7. RBAC: using the documented `--trusted-proxy`+XFF technique with a camera-scoped test user, confirm POST to /api/events/{id}/feedback 404s for an event on a camera outside the user's allow-list, and that a Viewer-role token gets 403 (Operator-minimum default, no auth.rs change needed).

P2.5-clip-attribute-facets.security_rbac_notes: GET /api/attributes and GET /api/search/by-attr both default to Viewer under auth.rs's method-based RBAC (Method::GET -> Role::Viewer, auth.rs:172-175) with no special-cased path override needed — matches /api/search and /api/events/{id}/similar today. search-by-attr MUST apply the same per-result camera scoping as event_similar/search_by_image (allowed_cameras() + camera_allowed() filtering the crop_embeddings() corpus scan results before returning, api.rs:3840-3876) so a camera-scoped Viewer can't discover events on cameras they can't see via attribute search — this is a real leak vector if skipped, since crop_embeddings() itself is un-scoped (all cameras). The attr_like AlarmRule condition needs no additional RBAC (alarm rules are already Admin/Operator-managed via the existing add_alarm_api/put_alarm_api gating) but validate_alarm_rule() should reject an unknown/malformed attr_like key (400) rather than silently accepting a value that will later resolve to None and skip the rule in fire_prompt_alarms — an honest-failure principle the codebase applies elsewhere (validate_alarm_rule already does this for action kinds/modes/days).
P2.5-clip-attribute-facets.validation_plan:
1. Build gate: `cargo clippy --all-targets -D warnings` + `cargo test -p zoomy` (new attributes.rs unit tests for find/prompt_for; db.rs unit test for attr_like round-tripping through is_prompt_rule/matches/matches_prompt/schedule_json parity with the existing prompt_like test at db.rs~4452); `cd web && npx tsc --noEmit && npm run build`.
2. Stop the running release NVR, rebuild release, restart via a detached PowerShell Start-Process (per project memory GOTCHA — never a timed background shell), wait ~90s for health.
3. Read-path (no new events needed — crop_embeddings already has months of real traffic from cameras 3/4/5/6/8): `curl :8080/api/attributes` -> confirm catalog JSON + `available:true` (CLIP models already present at repo root, confirmed via clip_vision.onnx/clip_text.onnx/clip_tokenizer.json). `curl ':8080/api/search/by-attr?key=vehicle_red&limit=20'` -> confirm ranked results; open the top hit's `/api/snapshots/{snapshot}` and visually confirm it's plausibly a reddish vehicle (spot-check 2-3 color facets this way, including one expected to have poor/no matches, e.g. a color unlikely to appear on-site, to confirm it degrades to low-similarity results rather than erroring).
4. Events page in Chrome (headless or interactive) at :8080: confirm the new 'Attributes:' chip row renders, click a chip -> list narrows via searchResults (same UX as existing smart-search), confirm it composes with the camera/time filters already applied on top of searchResults, confirm Clear resets it.
5. Alarms page: create a rule with only `attr_like` set (e.g. \"vehicle_black\", common enough to fire organically) + a webhook target pointing at a local test receiver (or ntfy), save, confirm `GET /api/alarms` shows attr_like persisted (round-tripped through schedule_json, matching the live-validation technique already used for zone_like/prompt_like/confirm_label). Confirm the rules-table 'candidates for the AI check' aiGated label appears for this rule.
6. Fire-path: either wait for organic matching traffic (watch server logs for the existing `\"prompt rule fired\"` tracing line, generalized to cover attr rules) and confirm the webhook payload's caption reads \"Matched \"...\" (NN% similar)\", OR — since a real color match can't be forced on demand — accept that full E2E fire validation may need a patient live wait (same honest limitation the project has previously logged for P2.2 prompt rules: \"temp rules armed awaiting validation\"), and treat the RBAC-scoped read-path curl checks (step 3) plus the unit-tested match/gate logic (step 1) as the primary pre-merge confidence signal for the fire path.
7. RBAC spot-check: hit `/api/search/by-attr` with a per-camera-scoped Operator/Viewer token (LAN IP, not loopback, per project's established RBAC-testing technique) and confirm results only include that user's allowed cameras.
P2.5-clip-attribute-facets.summary: P2.5 CLIP attribute facets is a near-verbatim generalization of the already-shipped P2.2 prompt-based rule mechanism (AlarmRule.prompt_like / is_prompt_rule / matches_prompt / fire_prompt_alarms in pipeline.rs), which already cosine-compares a CLIP text embedding against every detection's crop_embedding. The minimal-correct build is: (1) a new leaf module crates/core/src/attributes.rs holding a small curated catalog of canned CLIP prompts (vehicle colors + clothing colors; vehicle type and make/model deliberately excluded), (2) generalize AlarmRule's prompt machinery in db.rs so a new attr_like: Option<String> field (storing a catalog key, persisted in the existing schedule_json blob exactly like zone_like/vlm_prompt/prompt_like — no migration) resolves to a prompt and flows through the *same* fire_prompt_alarms path in pipeline.rs, (3) two new read-only endpoints in api.rs (GET /api/attributes for the catalog, GET /api/search/by-attr for the Events filter) that are near-copies of the existing event_similar/search_by_image handlers — same RBAC (allowed_cameras/camera_allowed), same spawn_blocking corpus-scan-of-crop_embeddings pattern, and (4) UI wiring: an 'Attributes:' TogglePill row on Events.tsx next to the existing 'Explore:' row (reusing the searchResults plumbing already built for text/photo search), and a grouped <select> in Alarms.tsx's 'Advanced conditions' disclosure next to the existing promptLike field, following its aiGated/matchPreview-exclusion precedent exactly. High-contention files touched are api.rs, db.rs, pipeline.rs, and api.ts, but every change to them is small and closely pattern-following (no restructuring). The two only-fully-explicit AlarmRule struct literals (db.rs:4454, notify.rs:515) will need attr_like: None added or the build breaks. No DB migration, no new ML model, no new runtime dependency — the crop embeddings this feature reads are already computed for every detection today.

P2.11.summary: Cammy today has two disconnected notification paths: (A) AlarmRule fires (crates/core/src/notify.rs fire()/fire_action(), called from 10 sites across pipeline.rs x3, audio.rs, api.rs x3, genai.rs, onvif_events.rs, posture.rs, transcribe.rs) dispatch fixed per-rule webhook/mqtt/ntfy/email targets with zero per-user awareness and never write to the notifications table; (B) system events (camera_offline, tamper, anomaly, absence, digest, etc.) call db.add_notification() and push.rs polls that table, fanning every row out to EVERY row in push_subscriptions with no per-user targeting at all (push_subscriptions has no user_id column). Building the per-user notification matrix means bridging these: attribute each WebPush subscription to the logged-in user (Principal.user_id, already injected by auth middleware), add a sparse notify_prefs table (user_id, rule_id[0=default], channel[push|email], enabled) following the exact 'empty = unrestricted' philosophy already used by user_cameras (#66), and make notify::fire() the single choke point that (1) writes an alarm-tagged row into an extended notifications table so it now also appears in the in-app bell and becomes push-fannable, and (2) synchronously emails each eligible user directly via the existing private email() helper (already keyed by an arbitrary target string, so a per-user address drops in with zero signature change). Camera scoping is NOT a new independent axis in v0 — it rides the rule's own existing camera_id plus the user's existing #66 camera RBAC scope (db-side equivalent of api.rs's allowed_cameras/camera_allowed), keeping the matrix a tractable 2D (user x rule) grid rather than 3D. Because fire() is called from 10 places, the DB reference needed for all of this is threaded through as one new fire(..., db: &Db) parameter — mechanical 1-line edits at each site (db is already in scope everywhere, confirmed by adjacent r.confirm_ok(db, cam.id, now) calls) — rather than restructuring the AlarmEvent struct (camera_id for RBAC checks is derived once inside fire() via the existing db.get_event(ev.event_id).camera_id lookup, avoiding any AlarmEvent/VlmGateJob field additions). Settings UI mirrors the already-shipped per-user camera-scope panel in UsersCard almost verbatim.

P2.11.descope_recommendation: v0 (ship this): (1) DB: users.email, push_subscriptions.user_id, notifications.rule_id/camera_id, new notify_prefs table. (2) notify::fire() gains a `db: &Db` parameter (touches all 10 call sites — pipeline.rs x3, audio.rs, api.rs x3, genai.rs, onvif_events.rs, posture.rs, transcribe.rs — each a 1-line arg addition; the 3 api.rs sites run inside tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || ...) closures and need `let db = st.db.clone();` added before the closure so it can be moved in). Inside fire(), after the existing action loop: derive camera_id via db.get_event(ev.event_id) (no AlarmEvent/VlmGateJob struct changes needed), write one add_alarm_notification row (feeds both the in-app bell AND push.rs's cursor), then loop db.list_users() and for each user check user_can_see_camera + notify_pref_enabled(uid, rule.id, "email"), calling the existing private email() fn with target=user.email when eligible. (3) push.rs's fan-out loop gains: look up each subscription's user_id, and when the notification carries a rule_id, gate on notify_pref_enabled(uid, rule_id, "push") + camera visibility (system notifications with rule_id=NULL keep today's unrestricted behavior). (4) API: /api/users/{id}/notify-prefs GET/PUT, users.email settable via PATCH /api/users/{id} and self-service, push_subscribe records user_id. (5) Settings UI: a per-user 'Notify' button next to the existing 'Cameras' button opening an inline panel — a global push/email toggle plus a per-rule override list. DEFER to v1: an independent per-camera dimension in notify_prefs for globally-scoped (camera_id=None) rules — v0 camera-awareness rides entirely on the rule's own camera_id plus the user's existing camera RBAC scope, keeping the matrix 2D (user x rule) instead of 3D. Also defer: letting system (non-alarm) notifications respect per-user prefs — v0 only gates alarm-rule-originated notifications.
P2.11.security_rbac_notes: Reuses the existing #66 per-camera RBAC primitive (user_cameras / allowed_cameras) as the visibility gate for both push and email fan-out — a user must already be able to see the camera (or be Admin/unrestricted) before receiving a notification about it, otherwise this would be a camera-scope bypass. The new /api/users/{id}/notify-prefs endpoints fall under the existing `path.starts_with("/api/users")` Admin-only gate in auth::min_role_for (auth.rs:161) with zero changes required — verify this is actually desired (see open_questions) rather than assumed. push_subscribe already sits at Viewer (auth.rs:150) which is correct for a user enabling push on their own device; attributing the subscription to p.user_id is server-derived from the session (not client input), so a Viewer cannot register a subscription "as" another user. notify_pref_enabled's SQL must correctly scope by user_id only — a malformed WHERE could leak or misapply another user's default row. Per-user email reuses the existing lettre/SMTP transport (notify.rs build_smtp, 10s timeout already in place) — the SMTP relay itself stays admin-configured infrastructure; a per-user email address flowing into to() should keep the existing parse-and-reject-on-error handling (notify.rs:304-311).
P2.11.validation_plan: Against the running NVR on :8080 (7 cams, 5 enabled: 3,4,5,6,8): (1) Migration sanity: after rebuilding, `sqlite3 data/zoomy.db '.schema notify_prefs'` / `.schema users` / `.schema push_subscriptions` / `.schema notifications` to confirm columns landed additively with no data loss. (2) Create two named users via Settings -> Users & roles (one Admin, one Viewer scoped to a subset of the 5 enabled cameras via the existing Cameras button — reuse #66 UI). Give the Viewer an email address and, in a second browser profile/incognito logged in as that Viewer, subscribe to WebPush so push_subscriptions.user_id gets set — confirm via `SELECT user_id FROM push_subscriptions` it's non-NULL. (3) In the new Notify matrix panel, turn OFF push for one specific alarm rule for the Viewer, leave email ON; trigger that rule (Alarms page Test button, or a real detection on cam 3/4/5/6/8) — confirm: the Viewer's browser does NOT get a WebPush toast (push off) but DOES get an email, and the Admin's default-enabled subscription DOES get the push. (4) RBAC negative test: scope the Viewer AWAY from a camera, fire an alarm rule on that camera, confirm neither push nor email reaches the Viewer even with prefs enabled. (5) Confirm the in-app Notifications bell now shows a row for the alarm fire with correct rule_id/camera_id via `SELECT * FROM notifications ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5` (previously alarm fires never appeared there). (6) Regression: trigger a non-alarm system notification (e.g. unplug a camera for camera_offline via health.rs) and confirm it still reaches every subscriber unconditionally. (7) tsc + vite build green; cargo clippy -D warnings + cargo test green (add unit tests for notify_pref_enabled's fallback precedence and user_can_see_camera's Admin-bypass, mirroring the existing armed_in_mode/push_allowed pure-function tests already in notify.rs's #[cfg(test)] block).
open_questions: Should system (non-alarm) notifications — camera_offline/online, tamper, anomaly, absence, digest, genai_error, backup — also respect notify_prefs (via the rule_id=0 global-default row), or stay unrestricted-to-everyone-subscribed as today? v0 descope leaves them unrestricted; confirm that's acceptable.

Per-camera dimension: is (user x rule) sufficient, or does the matrix genuinely need an independent camera axis even for globally-scoped (camera_id=None) rules? If yes, notify_prefs needs a 3rd key column and the UI needs a 3rd dimension — bumps this from L toward XL.
Email channel needs a per-user email address; is it acceptable to add users.email as a new admin-settable/self-settable field, or should v0 skip email entirely and ship push-only?

Should a Viewer be allowed to self-manage their own notify-prefs (a genuine self-service /api/me/notify-prefs), or is this strictly Admin-configured (simpler, matches how #66 user_cameras was scoped Admin-only)?

Is synchronous per-user email dispatch inside notify::fire() (on the detection/audio/pipeline thread) acceptable given N users could mean N SMTP round-trips per fire, or should per-user email be deferred to an async worker instead?

P3.4-homekit-bridge-v0.descope_recommendation: Ship v0 as: (1) a single global Settings.homekit_enabled toggle, Admin-gated; (2) a per-camera DetectConfig.homekit_expose opt-in (cheap — reuses the existing generic PATCH plumbing, and closes a real privacy gap for no_clip cameras); (3) a Cammy-generated, Settings-stored PIN shown as plain text (NO QR code — Apple Home accepts manual entry, and generating/rendering a scannable HAP setup-code QR is extra frontend work with no functional payoff for a first cut); (4) go2rtc config generation that preserves the (as-yet-unconfirmed) device_id/device_private/pairings fields verbatim across every regeneration, since Cammy's write_config fully rewrites data/go2rtc.yaml on every camera CRUD/settings save today (go2rtc.rs:231-262) and losing that persisted identity would silently break every existing pairing. EXPLICITLY DROP from v0, not just defer: the event->characteristic glue (MotionSensor bridging). Binary-string analysis of the vendored bin/go2rtc.exe (1.9.14) pkg/homekit only surfaces AV-bridge symbols (homekit.Server/ServerHandler/ServerPair/ServerProxy/hapHandler/trackToAudio/trackToVideo/audioToMedia/videoToMedia) and a Client/Consumer direction for CONSUMING other HomeKit cameras as sources — no custom-characteristic or non-Camera-service symbols were found, so go2rtc's built-in HAP module most likely cannot host a MotionSensor accessory at all. Doing that would require a second, independent Rust HAP server (e.g. the `hap` crate) — a materially larger, unproven-dependency effort that deserves its own separate spike/ticket, not a 'batch 2' bolt-on to this one. Also drop: any live 'N devices paired' telemetry richness beyond a configured/not-configured status (go2rtc's exact pairing-introspection API is unconfirmed).

P3.4-homekit-bridge-v0.security_rbac_notes: HomeKit/HAP pairing structurally bypasses Cammy's own per-camera RBAC (allowed_cameras/require_camera scoping in auth.rs, used by every other camera-scoped endpoint) — go2rtc's HAP bridge has no concept of Cammy users, roles, or per-camera permissions; once a device pairs (using only the PIN, over the LAN), it gets standing access to every exposed camera's live video regardless of the pairing device owner's Cammy role, and that access persists (via go2rtc's persisted pairings) even if the Cammy account that enabled it is later demoted or removed. Concretely this means: (1) toggling Settings.homekit_enabled must be Admin-only in put_settings, added to the existing Admin-diff-guard (api.rs:5404-5426) alongside auth_proxy_*/offsite_* — an Operator must not be able to silently open a new LAN video-exposure surface; (2) GET /api/homekit/status must be explicitly Admin-gated in auth.rs::min_role_for (the default GET->Viewer fallback would otherwise hand every Viewer the pairing PIN, letting them self-pair and get live video of cameras their Cammy role may not even list); (3) per-camera opt-in (DetectConfig.homekit_expose, default false) is recommended as a hard requirement, not an enhancement — without it, enabling HomeKit would silently blanket-expose every enabled camera including ones marked no_clip for nursery/bedroom/bathroom privacy (db.rs:257-263), directly undermining that feature's stated dignity guarantee; (4) the Settings card copy should carry an explicit callout (matching the project's severity/liability-disclaimer convention used for offsite backup and the residential safety features) stating in plain language that anyone who learns the PIN on the LAN gets live video, independent of their Cammy login.
P3.4-homekit-bridge-v0.summary: P3.4 v0 is "make Cammy's existing supervised go2rtc child (crates/core/src/go2rtc.rs) also run as a local HAP accessory bridge, live-view only." Binary-string analysis of the already-vendored bin/go2rtc.exe (v1.9.14, present in-repo though gitignored) confirms it ships a real HAP server (pkg/homekit: Server/ServerHandler/ServerPair/ServerProxy, mDNS `_hap._tcp.local.` advertisement, a web-UI pairing panel with a PIN/QR, and a config struct with yaml tags pin/listen/device_id/device_private/pairings) — so docs/06's thesis ("go2rtc does AV; event->characteristic glue is the new work") is directionally correct, and the "go2rtc does AV" half really is close to free. The implementation is: extend go2rtc.rs's already-established config-generation pattern (write_config, called only from restart_with/sync_streams) to also emit a `homekit:` block, gated by a new global Settings.homekit_enabled + a new per-camera DetectConfig.homekit_expose (both plain JSON-blob fields, zero migrations, following the exact no_clip/two_way_audio/pose_detect precedent). The one genuinely new piece of engineering is that Cammy's write_config today unconditionally regenerates the ENTIRE go2rtc.yaml from the camera registry on every restart/CRUD — but HAP pairing state (device_id/device_private/pairings) is almost certainly persisted by go2rtc back into that same file, so a naive regenerate would silently break every existing Apple Home pairing on the next camera edit or settings save. The fix is a small, self-contained "preserve the trailing homekit: block verbatim across regenerations" routine (new crates/core/src/homekit.rs), which needs a hands-on spike against the real go2rtc.exe (already present) before being trusted. Because a HomeKit pairing is authenticated only by a PIN and completely bypasses Cammy's own per-camera RBAC (auth.rs's allowed_cameras/require_camera), the toggle and the new status endpoint that surfaces the PIN both need Admin-only gating, following the exact auth_proxy_*/offsite_* precedent already in put_settings and the /api/backup precedent in auth::min_role_for. The web side is two small, template-following additions: a Settings card (modeled byte-for-byte on the Offsite-backup card + OffsiteStatusReadout) and a per-camera toggle in Cameras.tsx's TuneModal. Event->characteristic glue (a MotionSensor HAP characteristic) is recommended to be dropped from v0 scope entirely, not deferred as a stretch goal within it: the binary strings show go2rtc's HAP module only implements the AV/Camera-service direction, with no custom-characteristic symbols, so that work would require a second, independent Rust HAP server dependency and is honestly a separate, larger effort. Real Apple Home hardware pairing cannot be exercised in this environment and must be handed to a human tester with an explicit checklist.
P3.4-homekit-bridge-v0.validation_plan: 1) SPIKE FIRST, before touching Rust: hand-copy data/go2rtc.yaml to a scratch file, append a candidate `homekit:` block (guessed pin/listen keys), run `./bin/go2rtc.exe -config <scratch>.yaml` directly (outside Cammy, on a spare port) and observe: does it start a HAP listener (netstat/log line), does it advertise `_hap._tcp.local.` via mDNS, does its own web UI (http://127.0.0.1:<scratch-api-port>/) show a working pairing QR/PIN panel, and — critically — does it rewrite the scratch config file in place after a real pairing attempt. This settles the open schema/persistence questions before go2rtc.rs's generator is written. 2) Build/lint: cargo build -p zoomy, cargo clippy --all-targets -D warnings, cargo test (new homekit.rs unit tests: desired_homekit_cameras filtering, block-preserve/replace logic, plus the two updated db.rs round-trip literals). 3) Live against the running release NVR on :8080 (7 cameras, per the standing test rig): toggle Settings.homekit_enabled on via the new UI card as Admin, confirm data/go2rtc.yaml gained a homekit: block and go2rtc's process log shows the HAP server starting (detached Start-Process restart per the project's standing GOTCHA — do not use a timed background shell); then make an UNRELATED settings save (e.g. flip highlight_motion) and confirm the homekit: block's device_id/device_private/pairings text is byte-identical before and after — this is the critical regression the whole design exists to prevent. 4) RBAC: call GET /api/homekit/status as Admin (200, PIN present) and as a Viewer/Operator token (403); attempt PUT /api/settings with homekit_enabled flipped as a non-Admin role-scoped token over --trusted-proxy (403, mirroring the existing offsite/auth_proxy test in the round-2 session notes). 5) PATCH /api/cameras/{id} with detect_config.homekit_expose=true on exactly one camera, confirm go2rtc restarts (log line) and the newly-exposed camera's live WebRTC tile survives the expected brief restart blip while an unrelated camera's tile is otherwise undisturbed. 6) HAND TO A HUMAN — cannot be done in this environment: on the real LAN, open the iPhone/iPad Apple Home app, Add Accessory, enter the displayed PIN manually, confirm the camera tile appears and streams live video, and confirm it still works (no re-pair needed) after a full Cammy restart — this is the actual feature promise and the step that determines whether the whole v0 is real.

P3.7.summary: P3.7 records go2rtc's existing low-res sub-stream (`{name}_sub`, already published whenever `Camera.detect_source` is set — go2rtc.rs `desired_streams` line 38-47) to disk alongside the main stream, using a second, fully-reused `recorder::Recording::start` ffmpeg -c-copy process (crates/recorder needs zero changes). The core work is: (1) a `segments.stream` column (default 'main') plus threading a `stream` parameter through db.rs's `upsert_segment`/`find_segment_at`/`list_segments` — required because main+sub segments share near-identical `start_ts`, so the existing `ORDER BY start_ts DESC LIMIT 1` in `find_segment_at` would otherwise pick either ambiguously; (2) refactoring record.rs's reconcile loop (`running` HashMap, `desired` set, retention `dirs` lists) from keying by camera_id alone to keying by (camera_id, stream), so a sub-stream desired-set (gated on a new per-camera `DetectConfig.record_substream` bool AND a non-empty `detect_source`) gets started/stopped/indexed/pruned the same way main already is — including extending BOTH the byte-cap prune dirs and the per-camera age-prune dirs to cover the new `.../sub/` subdirectory, or sub-stream files silently never get pruned; (3) extending the 3 already-shipped recording endpoints (`/api/recordings`, `/api/recordings/at`, `/api/recordings/{id}/thumb.jpg`) with a `stream` query param / internal lo-res lookup — no new routes; (4) a per-camera opt-in toggle in Cameras.tsx's TuneModal (same tab as the existing sub-stream URL field) and a manual quality selector wired into CameraDetail's unified playback bar (the flagship player), with SequencePlayer/Events viewers deliberately deferred. Event-clip extraction (`extract_event_clip`'s 4 `find_segment_at` call sites) stays pinned to \"main\" always, so evidence quality is never silently degraded.

P3.7.descope_recommendation: Ship the full backend correctness story (schema, dual recorder-loop reconcile, retention parity across both streams, offsite exclusion) plus a manual quality selector wired into ONE player — CameraDetail, the flagship unified live↔playback view — as v0. Defer to v1: (1) the Recordings-page SequencePlayer quality toggle (its 2-slot rolling-queue architecture means every queued clip would need sub-stream re-resolution, a second full pass of similar-sized work to the CameraDetail wiring); (2) Events.tsx event-clip viewers gaining a quality option (arguably out of scope forever — event evidence should stay hi-res by default); (3) any real adaptive-BITRATE logic (auto-switching on measured bandwidth) — v0's 'quality selector' is a manual High/Low toggle only, not automatic; (4) surfacing sub-stream recording health on the StatusBoard/Live grid. The single highest-risk item to get right in v0 is the record.rs reconcile-loop refactor (HashMap keyed by camera_id → keyed by (camera_id, stream)) and its retention-dir fan-out (age-prune + byte-cap-prune dirs list must include each camera's `sub` subdirectory) — get this wrong and sub-stream files either never get pruned (silent disk-fill) or the whole reconcile loop regresses main-stream recording, which would be a severe regression on a stability-critical subsystem. If time is tight, the honest cut is: ship backend dual recording + retention correctness + the `/api/recordings` stream param, but hold the CameraDetail quality-selector UI for a fast v1 follow-up commit — the backend alone is independently useful (verifiable via curl) and de-risks the harder half first.

open_questions: Should `record_substream` require `detect_source` to be set at save-time (server-side validation, e.g. `patch_camera` rejects the combination) or just silently no-op in record.rs's reconcile loop when the sub-stream role is absent? Recommend the latter (fail-open, consistent with how `two_way_audio`/`pose_detect` degrade gracefully when their prerequisite is missing) but surface it in the UI as a disabled toggle + hint, not a hard validation error.

Should the per-camera retention override (`DetectConfig.retention_days`) apply identically to both streams, or should the sub-stream get a separate (likely shorter) retention window since it's meant for cheap scrubbing, not evidence? v0 plan applies the SAME `days` to both dirs via `recorder::prune(&[main_dir, sub_dir], Some(days), None)` — simplest and consistent with 'a lightweight copy alongside', but worth confirming since sub-stream could otherwise silently double a camera's effective disk footprint.

Enhanced-retention re-encoding (`reduction_candidates`) is scoped to 'main' only in this plan — should low-res sub-stream segments EVER be re-encoded/shrunk further, or are they already small enough to skip entirely? Recommend skip (current plan) since the sub-stream's whole purpose is already being small.

`motion_search` (api.rs 4315-4412) is left hardcoded to `stream="main"` in this plan since `motion_grid` isn't stream-scoped — confirm that's acceptable (motion masks are computed from whatever source `detect_source`/main feeds the pipeline, which is architecturally separate from what gets recorded).

This plan implements manual-only; true bandwidth-adaptive auto-switching is flagged as an XL follow-up, not v0.

The one thing worth double-checking in implementation: validate the incoming `stream` string against an enum (`"main"`/`"sub"` only, else 400) before it reaches the SQL parameter — it's already parameterized (no injection risk via rusqlite params!), but an unvalidated arbitrary string would just silently return zero rows rather than erroring, which is a minor UX/debuggability issue, not a security one.

save. 2) Watch `data/recordings/<camera>/sub/` on disk for new strftime-named segments appearing alongside the existing `data/recordings/<camera>/*.mp4` main segments, confirm both keep advancing (ffmpeg processes alive) via `Get-Process ffmpeg` showing two children per dual-stream camera. 3) `curl http://localhost:8080/api/recordings?camera_id=<id>&stream=sub` (with a session cookie/loopback) — expect segment rows with `stream:\"sub\"`, distinct `path`s under `.../sub/`. 4) `curl .../api/recordings/at?camera_id=<id>&ts=<now-30>&stream=sub` vs `...&stream=main` (or omitted) — expect two DIFFERENT segment ids resolving to the same instant, one much smaller in `bytes`/lower resolution when played. 5) In Chrome, open CameraDetail for that camera, seek the timeline into playback, flip the new quality selector to Low — verify the `<video src>` swaps to a `/api/recordings/<sub-id>/video` URL and visibly drops resolution; flip back to High and confirm it restores the original hi-res segment; test the fallback path by seeking to a moment BEFORE the sub-stream toggle was enabled (no sub coverage) — expect a graceful toast + stay-on-High, not a broken player. 6) Confirm `/api/recordings/<main-id>/thumb.jpg` on the Recordings scrub grid still renders correctly and check server logs / a timing comparison that thumb extraction for a covered instant is now reading the smaller sub file (e.g. temporarily log the chosen source path, or diff extraction latency against a same-camera non-dual-stream segment). 7) Let retention run one full `RETENTION_EVERY` cycle (60s) after lowering `retention_days` for that camera to something tiny in a scratch/test pass — confirm files under both `.../<camera>/` and `.../<camera>/sub/` get pruned and their `segments` rows disappear (not just the main dir). 8) If offsite backup is enabled in this environment, confirm via `GET /api/offsite/status` or DB inspection that `pending_offsite` is NOT queuing the new `sub`-stream segments. 9) `cargo clippy --all-targets -D warnings`, `cargo test -p zoomy` (or whatever the core package test target is) green, `cd web && npm run build` (tsc+vite) green, before/after the live checks.

P2.16.summary: P2.16 (Object lifecycle detail view) turns out to be almost pure plumbing because every prerequisite already exists in code but was never wired end to end: tracker::Track (crates/tracker/src/lib.rs) already carries a stable id and a bounded history: VecDeque<(ts,x,y)> trajectory; crates/core/src/analytics.rs's Crossing/Loiter and residential.rs's ResEvent already carry track_id — it is simply dropped at the pipeline.rs emit_analytics_event call sites instead of being persisted. The minimal correct build is: (1) thread track_id + a serde_json snapshot of the firing Track's history through emit_analytics_event's existing 5 call sites and 2 new nullable events columns (track_id, path_json) in db.rs, reusing add_event_dir; (2) a new read-only GET /api/events/{id}/lifecycle that aggregates same-(camera_id,track_id) events ordered by ts — pure SQL aggregation, no new worker/table, mirroring event_similar's shape and get_event_api's RBAC scoping; (3) a web 'Track' button + step-list modal on the Events viewer, built by copying the existing Similar-button/modal pattern verbatim (same debounce-token state, same click-a-row-to-reopen-the-viewer click-to-seek trick — zero new video-seeking code needed since Events.tsx's open-event effect already resolves the covering recording). This also quietly ships docs/08's never-completed P1.5 'path_json persist', which P2.16 was documented as depending on. The one real correctness trap found during recon: per-camera track ids reset to 1 on every service restart (in-memory HashMap<i64,Tracker> is rebuilt fresh at startup), so the new lifecycle query must be temporally gap-bounded or it can splice two unrelated objects' events into one fabricated narrative after a restart.

Seed event's camera-scoped narrative: fetches the event, require_camera(allowed_cameras, ev.camera_id) exactly like get_event_api/event_similar, then if event.track_id is None returns {available:false}; else calls db.track_lifecycle(camera_id, track_id) and returns {available:true, track_id, steps: Event[] (ts ASC), path: [ts,x,y][] (the most recent non-empty track_path among the steps)}. No new write surface, no new RBAC surface — reuses require_camera verbatim.
db_changes: events.track_id INTEGER (nullable, additive ALTER TABLE) — the physical-track join key; per-camera-scoped u64 Tracker id cast to i64. Set only on tracker-driven narrative events (crossing/wrong_way/loiter/zone_enter/child/fall/still_water); NULL on ordinary per-frame detections, occupancy, package, tamper, camera_* (ONVIF) events.

events.path_json TEXT (nullable, additive ALTER TABLE) — JSON-serialized snapshot of the firing Track's `history: VecDeque<(i64,f32,f32)>` at emit time (serde_json::to_string(&t.history), reusing Track's existing #[derive(Serialize)] — no new serialization code). Bounded to Tracker::history_len (120 points), so a compact per-event blob.
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS events_track ON events(camera_id, track_id, ts) — cheap, matches the events_cam_label_ts precedent (db.rs line 1365); speeds the new track_lifecycle query.

New query fn Db::track_lifecycle(&self, camera_id: i64, track_id: i64) -> Result<Vec<Event>> in db.rs (place it right after get_event, ~line 1805): same 4-column-extended SELECT as list_events/get_event/unknown_gait_events, WHERE camera_id=?1 AND track_id=?2 ORDER BY ts ASC. IMPORTANT correctness note: track ids are small per-camera-Tracker counters that RESET on every service restart (Tracker::new sets next_id=1, and the in-memory `trackers: HashMap<i64,Tracker>` in pipeline.rs line 161 is recreated fresh each run) — an unbounded WHERE camera_id=?/track_id=? query can conflate a reused low id from BEFORE a restart with an unrelated one AFTER it. The read path (db fn or the API handler) must clip the result to a bounded temporal cluster around the seed event — e.g. walk outward from the seed row and stop at the first gap > ~10 min between consecutive same-track_id rows — rather than returning every row that ever shared that (camera_id, track_id) pair.

P2.16.descope_recommendation: Not L/XL, but the honest v0 slice is still worth stating since two roadmap items (docs/08 P1.5's 'path_json persist' and P2.16 itself) collapse into one build: v0 = (1) thread track_id (already computed) through emit_analytics_event's 5 call sites for crossing/wrong_way/loiter/zone_enter/child/fall/still_water only — leave occupancy/package/tamper/ordinary-detection events untouched (they have no single owning track or would need new IoU-matching work); (2) persist path_json as a raw snapshot of Track.history at emit time — no retirement hook, no background worker; (3) one new read-only /lifecycle endpoint aggregating same-(camera_id,track_id) events, gap-bounded to avoid the track-id-reuse-after-restart correctness trap; (4) a 'Track' button + step-list modal on the Events viewer only (not CameraDetail/Recordings). Deferred to v1: polyline-over-snapshot canvas rendering (Heatmap.tsx recipe is ready whenever wanted), back-filling track_id onto ordinary per-frame detection events via IoU-matching against confirmed tracks (stationary_should_fire is the template), and entry points from CameraDetail's event rail / CrossTimeline.
P2.16.security_rbac_notes: No new security surface: the sole new endpoint reuses require_camera(allowed_cameras(&st,&p)?, seed.camera_id) verbatim (the get_event_api/event_similar pattern), and the lifecycle DB query is itself constrained to that already-authorized camera_id, so a scoped Viewer/Operator token can't pivot to another camera's tracks. No new PII beyond what Event already exposes (label/zone/direction/anchor coordinates) — track_path is just frame-fraction trajectory points, same sensitivity class as the existing bbox/anchor fields already returned on every event. The one correctness (not security) risk to close before shipping: bound track_lifecycle's result set temporally (see db_changes note) so a low track_id reused across a service restart can't merge two unrelated objects' events into one narrative.
