P3.3-ha-integration.descope_recommendation: Split cleanly along the Rust/Python boundary. v0 (ship first, fully live-validatable against :8080): the SSE feed (GET /api/events/stream, tapping mqtt.rs's existing choke point with one broadcast::Sender, RBAC-filtered) PLUS inbound MQTT commands (arm/disarm + trigger/snapshot only, via a pure parse_command(prefix, topic, payload) -> Option<Command> in mqtt.rs, gated behind the new opt-in Settings.mqtt_commands_enabled, dispatched through extracted apply_arm_mode/fire_soft_trigger helpers so the HTTP handlers and the MQTT path share one code path). This alone is genuinely useful (any MQTT automation platform, not just HA, can now arm/trigger Cammy) and is M effort, fully testable end-to-end with mosquitto_pub/curl. v1 (defer): the HACS custom_components/cammy Python package. Ship only a thin skeleton in v0 if attempted at all — manifest.json + const.py + config_flow.py (host + a pasted API token, validated via GET /api/me) + __init__.py (one long-lived aiohttp SSE listener task) + ONE alarm_control_panel entity (arm/disarm, highest value, direct REST) + ONE aggregate binary_sensor driven off the SSE stream. Defer: per-camera/per-label entity proliferation, a proper DataUpdateCoordinator, options-flow, translations beyond en.json, device-registry polish, HACS-store submission + hassfest CI validation, and the button entity for trigger. The Python component cannot be live-validated in this environment at all (no Home Assistant instance anywhere in the repo/memory) — that whole track is inherently unvalidated until a real HA box is available, which argues for treating it as its own follow-up ticket rather than bundling it into this pass.
P3.3-ha-integration.summary: P3.3 splits into two very different halves. The Rust backend half (SSE feed + inbound MQTT commands) is a clean, minimal-diff M-effort change because the codebase already has a single system-wide event choke point: mqtt::EventMsg, cloned as an mpsc::Sender into every event producer (pipeline.rs, audio.rs, onvif_events.rs, posture.rs, notify.rs::fire_action, and 3 api.rs handlers) and consumed by exactly one mpsc::Receiver inside mqtt::run (mqtt.rs:130-332). Rather than touching every producer, add a tokio::sync::broadcast::Sender<EventMsg> that mqtt.rs re-broadcasts to on every message it already receives (including the MQTT-off drop path, which today silently discards events) — that single tap point becomes GET /api/events/stream, RBAC-filtered per Principal exactly like list_events. Inbound MQTT commands (arm/disarm/trigger/snapshot) are new: despite docs/08 marking P1.11 ('MQTT-subscribe trigger source') as a precedent, it was never actually built — no /api/trigger route exists and mqtt.rs's connection-driver thread today ignores every incoming publish. Build it by having mqtt.rs subscribe to a wildcard command topic (opt-in, gated on a new Settings.mqtt_commands_enabled, default off) and forward parsed commands over a new tokio mpsc channel to an async consumer task (spawned in lib.rs, holding a clone of AppState) that calls two extracted, HTTP-handler-and-MQTT-shared functions: apply_arm_mode (pulled out of set_arm_mode, api.rs:4743-4784) and fire_soft_trigger (pulled out of soft_trigger, api.rs:2522-2662). The HACS custom_components/cammy Python package is a separate, much larger and differently-risked effort (new ecosystem, no test harness in this repo/session, cannot be live-validated against the running NVR the way the Rust half can) — recommend descoping it to its own follow-up per the descope_recommendation field.

open_questions: Should Settings.mqtt_commands_enabled be added to put_settings' Admin-only field list (api.rs:5410-5419), matching the auth_proxy_*/offsite_* precedent? It grants a new remote-control surface, but mqtt_url itself (which is arguably just as sensitive — repointing the broker) is currently Operator-writable, so there's an existing inconsistency either way this is decided.

MQTT inbound commands run with NO per-camera RBAC and no session/token concept at all — trust is 100% delegated to 'whoever can publish to the configured broker'. On a shared home-automation broker (the common HA setup) that may include other integrations/devices the owner didn't intend to grant arm/disarm/trigger power to. Worth a loud callout/caveat in the Settings toggle copy (drafted into the web_changes item) rather than a code gate, since there's no natural finer-grained control without inventing broker ACLs ourselves.

Command topic vocabulary: should parse_command accept ONLY Cammy's own words ('home'/'away'/'disarmed', matching /api/arm's existing vocabulary) or also HA's generic mqtt alarm_control_panel convention ('ARM_HOME'/'ARM_AWAY'/'DISARM', case-insensitive) for out-of-the-box compatibility with users who wire it up via HA's built-in generic MQTT platform instead of installing the custom component? Recommend supporting both (cheap, pure-fn, unit-testable) but flagging as a decision point.

Should the broadcast channel bound (proposed 256) and the SSE per-connection Lagged-handling (skip vs. drop-and-warn) be tuned/tested under a real detection burst? No load-test env for this is described anywhere in the repo/memory.

The HACS component cannot be live-validated in this environment (no Home Assistant instance exists anywhere in the repo, docs, or memory notes) — is a throwaway HA OS VM/container in scope for this pass, or is the Python track explicitly a separate follow-up ticket with its own validation session?

Reconnect timing: mqtt.rs only breaks/reconnects when `mqtt_url` changes (lib.rs mqtt.rs:303-305); flipping mqtt_commands_enabled on/off while already connected won't take effect until the next natural reconnect (matches existing behavior for mqtt_ha_prefix/mqtt_state_timeout_secs, but worth deciding whether to widen the reconnect-trigger condition).
P3.3-ha-integration.security_rbac_notes: Two very different trust models collide here and both need to be documented, not just implemented. (1) SSE feed: reuses the existing per-request Principal/allowed_cameras/require_camera RBAC (auth.rs) exactly like list_events — GET is Viewer-reachable by auth::min_role_for's default, and a camera-scoped Viewer/Operator token must only ever see EventMsgs for cameras in their allowed set (system-level messages like the 'mode' arm-state event, which carries camera: String::new(), are the one deliberate exception and should pass through to every authenticated subscriber). (2) Inbound MQTT commands: NOT scoped by the web RBAC system at all — arm/disarm/trigger fire with full trust once mqtt_commands_enabled is on, gated only by 'can this peer publish to the configured broker.' This mirrors the pre-existing trust posture of outbound MQTT (which already publishes every camera's events regardless of any user's RBAC scope) but is a new escalation from read-only to read-write. Mitigations already in place by design: opt-in default-off setting, and reuse of the exact same validation as the HTTP endpoints (apply_arm_mode/fire_soft_trigger reject bad mode strings / oversized or control-char labels, same as today). No new secrets are introduced. The HACS component's own auth (a pasted API token, #48) is scoped by that token's assigned role/camera-scope like any other Bearer caller — so the native-integration path is properly RBAC'd even though the raw-MQTT path is not; this asymmetry is worth calling out to users in the Settings toggle copy.
P3.3-ha-integration.validation_plan: 1) Build gate (no server stop needed): cargo build -p zoomy + cargo clippy -p zoomy --all-targets -- -D warnings + cargo test -p zoomy covering new unit tests for mqtt::parse_command (topic/payload -> Command mapping, both vocabularies if adopted, malformed/empty payload -> None) and a broadcast fan-out smoke test (send on the mpsc rx side, assert the same EventMsg arrives on a subscribed broadcast::Receiver). 2) Release rebuild + detached-process restart (per the project's standard GOTCHA — PowerShell Start-Process, ~90s downtime) to bring the running NVR on :8080 up to the new binary. 3) SSE live check: curl -N http://localhost:8080/api/events/stream from loopback (auto-admin) — confirm content-type: text/event-stream and periodic keep-alive; in a second terminal fire curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/cameras/<id>/trigger -d '{"label":"sse-test"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' and confirm a data: {...label:sse-test...} line appears on the open stream within ~1s. 4) RBAC check: mint an Operator API token scoped to a camera subset via PUT /api/users/{id}/cameras (existing #66 mechanism), open the SSE stream with Authorization: Bearer <token> from a non-loopback client (or --trusted-proxy + XFF like prior RBAC validations), trigger events on both an in-scope and an out-of-scope camera, confirm only the in-scope one appears on the stream. 5) MQTT inbound commands: PUT /api/settings with mqtt_commands_enabled:true and mqtt_url pointed at a local mosquitto broker (spin one up if none is already running — no existing broker is referenced anywhere in the test-environment memory notes, unlike the real Dahua/Amcrest cameras); mosquitto_pub -t zoomy/cmd/arm -m away then GET /api/arm confirms arm_mode flipped, and check the audit log / in-app notification fired same as a normal PUT would; mosquitto_pub -t zoomy/cmd/trigger/<camera-name> -m 'front door delivery' then GET /api/events?limit=1 confirms a new bookmarked event with that label and a snapshot exists, same shape as a soft_trigger via HTTP. 6) Regression: mosquitto_sub -t 'zoomy/#' during a live detection confirms outbound events/discovery topics are byte-identical to pre-change behavior (the new sse_tx.send is additive, must not alter existing publish content/ordering). 7) HACS skeleton: cannot be live-validated against :8080 in this environment — needs a real or containerized Home Assistant instance (none exists in this repo/session) to add the custom_components/cammy folder, run through config_flow with a freshly minted API token, and confirm the alarm_control_panel entity's arm/disarm calls actually flip Cammy's arm_mode and the binary_sensor updates off a live SSE event — flagged as an explicit follow-up validation session, not something this pass can close out end-to-end.

P2.9.summary: Add a new alarm-action kind "deterrence" (ONVIF relay-triggered siren/light) that reuses the entire existing ONVIF SOAP client (crates/core/src/ptz.rs: CamTarget/parse_source/soap_call/extract_between) and the entire existing action-dispatch chassis (crates/core/src/notify.rs: Action/AlarmEvent/fire/fire_action). v0 is deliberately RELAY-ONLY (SetRelayOutputState via ONVIF DeviceIO) — the "play WAV over two-way-audio backchannel" half of the spec is explicitly deferred (see descope) because no server-side (browser-less) audio-push path exists today; two_way_audio (db.rs:216-221) is 100% browser-mic-to-camera WebRTC, there is no go2rtc/ONVIF mechanism in this codebase for the SERVER to push a file to a camera speaker, and building+validating that blind would be the highest-risk, least-grounded part of the ask. New module crates/core/src/deterrence.rs provides probe()/trigger_relay() by copying ptz.rs's exact SOAP idioms (GetCapabilities→XAddr search mirrors ptz_xaddr's `:PTZ>` split for `:DeviceIO>`; token parsing mirrors profile_token's `token="..."` extraction; SetRelayOutputState mirrors continuous_move/stop). A new GET/POST /api/cameras/{id}/deter pair mirrors ptz_caps/ptz_command byte-for-byte (same RBAC via allowed_cameras/require_camera, same spawn_blocking wrapping). AlarmEvent gains two new fields — camera_source: Option<&str> (the firing camera's go2rtc source URL, so fire_action can resolve ONVIF creds) and deterrence_enabled: bool (mirrors settings.deterrence_enabled, a new global opt-in master kill-switch parallel to genai_enabled/transcription_enabled) — which the Rust compiler will force into every one of the ~13 existing AlarmEvent{} struct literals (pipeline.rs×4, audio.rs, onvif_events.rs, posture.rs, transcribe.rs, genai.rs, api.rs×3, notify.rs's own test), making the plumbing mechanical and impossible to silently miss a site. Arm-mode gating is FREE — every dispatch site already ANDs `notify::armed_in_mode(&r.modes, &settings.arm_mode)` before calling fire(), so a deterrence action inherits Home/Away/Disarmed scoping with zero new code. "Never auto-escalate" is honored by NOT building any escalation state machine — each Action fires independently, gated only by the rule's existing cooldown_secs/snooze_until (docs/08 line 111 explicitly defers "escalation ladder" to later). Honesty/fail-soft is delivered two ways: (1) GET /api/cameras/{id}/deter lets the Alarms rule-builder UI probe-and-show real relay tokens (or "no relay output found" / "camera has no ONVIF credentials") BEFORE a rule is ever armed, and (2) a global Settings.deterrence_enabled toggle (default OFF) with a loud callout means even a configured rule does nothing physical until the owner explicitly opts in, addressing "backchannel/siren sink has never been live-validated."

P2.9.descope_recommendation: Ship v0 as RELAY-ONLY (ONVIF DeviceIO SetRelayOutputState → an external siren/strobe/light box wired to the camera's alarm-out terminal) and explicitly DO NOT attempt the \"play WAV over two-way-audio backchannel\" half in this pass. Reasoning: the two-way-audio path today is 100% browser-driven (getUserMedia → WebRTC sendonly track through /api/ws, CameraDetail.tsx lines 43-102) — there is no existing go2rtc/ONVIF mechanism anywhere in this codebase for the SERVER PROCESS ITSELF to push a pre-recorded file into a camera's speaker without a browser in the loop, so \"build\" it it would mean inventing and validating a brand-new unvalidated media pipeline (likely an `ffmpeg:`-producer PUT to go2rtc's stream API with a `#backchannel=1` param, per go2rtc's own docs — untested here) — exactly the highest-risk, least-grounded part of the ask, on hardware (Dahua/Amcrest test cams) that may not even have a speaker. The ONVIF relay path, by contrast, reuses a SOAP client that's already shipped, tested, and live-fire-validated for PTZ (crates/core/src/ptz.rs) and for camera-side event ingestion (onvif_events.rs) — same host, same credential parsing, same XAddr-discovery idiom, just a different service/verb. Even the relay-only v0 stays large (L) purely from breadth: extending AlarmEvent forces mechanical edits across ~13 call sites in 8 files — but every edit is compiler-verified (miss one and cargo build fails immediately naming the exact literal), so the size is diff-count risk, not logic risk. A defensible v0.1 slice within even the relay-only scope, if L still feels too big: ship crates/core/src/deterrence.rs + the GET/POST /api/cameras/{id}/deter probe/test endpoints + the Settings.deterrence_enabled master switch + Alarms.tsx UI FIRST (fully useful on its own — a user can probe capabilities and manually fire a relay from a \"Test\" button), and defer wiring it into notify.rs::fire_action / AlarmEvent (i.e., defer making it an automatic ALARM RULE action) to a fast follow-up once relay hardware is confirmed to exist on a real test camera — this avoids threading camera_source through all 13 call sites until the actuation path itself is proven live.
P2.9.security_rbac_notes: New routes reuse the exact per-camera RBAC scoping already established for PTZ: `require_camera(&allowed_cameras(&st, &p)?, id)?` before any ONVIF call, so a sub-Admin user scoped to cameras [3,4,6] gets a 404 (not a leaked capability signal) on cameras 5/8, matching the ptz_caps precedent's own comment (\"so an out-of-scope camera doesn't leak its PTZ capability\", api.rs:1942-1943). Role tier: GET falls to the default Viewer (read-only capability probe is safe to expose broadly, same as PTZ), POST falls to the default Operator (a physical-world action, same tier as PTZ move commands) — no new entries needed in auth.rs::min_role_for (lines 139-176), it already defaults GET→Viewer/non-GET→Operator and neither /api/cameras/{id}/ptz nor the new /deter route needs special-casing. Accepted risk (matches existing PTZ precedent, not a new gap): any Operator-role user or Operator-scoped API token can trigger a real-world siren/light on cameras they're scoped to — this is the same trust tier PTZ already grants for physical camera movement, so it's consistent, not novel. The new global Settings.deterrence_enabled kill-switch rides the generic Settings PUT (Operator-writable, same as genai_enabled/transcription_enabled — NOT in the Admin-only auth_proxy_*/offsite_* carve-out at auth.rs:161-171), which is appropriate since it's a feature toggle, not an account-security field. No new secrets are introduced (ONVIF creds are already embedded in Camera.source and already flow through go2rtc.rs's `clean()` control-char stripping and the existing redact_url_creds masking for sub-Admin GET /api/cameras — unaffected by this feature). Recommend NOT logging the relay token or camera source at info level in deterrence.rs (mirror ptz.rs which never logs credentials); only camera name + relay token identifier (not host/creds) at info, full error detail at debug.
P2.9.validation_plan:
Against the running NVR on :8080 (7 cameras configured, 5 enabled: 3,4,5,6,8):
1. Probe-honesty pass (no hardware assumptions): for each enabled camera id, `Invoke-RestMethod http://localhost:8080/api/cameras/{id}/deter` (loopback = auth-exempt) and record which report relays vs `{relays:[], error:\"...\"}` vs an ONVIF-unreachable error — this is real live validation of the \"probe ONVIF, show what is/isn't available\" requirement even if every test camera turns out to have zero relay outputs (plausible for fixed IP cams with no alarm-out terminal — report that honestly rather than fabricate a positive result).
2. For any camera reporting ≥1 relay: `Invoke-RestMethod -Method Post http://localhost:8080/api/cameras/{id}/deter -Body '{\"token\":\"<token>\",\"active_secs\":2}' -ContentType application/json`; confirm a 200 (the camera ACCEPTED the SOAP command) and check server logs for the trigger_relay call — physically observing a wired siren/light is a bonus, not a blocker, if no relay hardware is actually wired to the test cameras.
3. End-to-end through the Alarms UI: open http://localhost:8080/#/alarms, edit/create a rule scoped to a camera with a probed relay, add a \"trigger siren/light (ONVIF)\" action, confirm the target dropdown shows the real probed token(s) (not a blind text box), save, click \"Test\" — verify the rule fires and the server log shows the deterrence dispatch attempt.
4. Master-switch honesty check: with Settings → Modes & alerts → \"Deterrence actions\" OFF, click Test again and confirm nothing is sent to the camera (log shows a \"deterrence disabled\" skip, not a SOAP call) — then flip it ON and confirm the SOAP call now fires. This is the critical proof that the feature is truly opt-in/fail-soft by default.
5. Negative-path: point a rule's camera at one added via plain `rtsp://host/path` (no `user:pass@`) and confirm `GET /api/cameras/{id}/deter` returns `available:false`-shaped honest output and the Alarms UI shows the \"no ONVIF credentials\" callout instead of an empty/broken dropdown.
6. RBAC: call `POST /api/cameras/{id}/deter` with a Viewer-role API token (expect 403) and with an Operator token scoped to a different camera set (expect 404 via require_camera), mirroring the RBAC live-validation technique already used for #66 (per-camera scoping) and #48 (API tokens) per CLAUDE.md's history.
7. No-stall check: fire a real detection-triggered alarm with a deterrence action on a live/busy camera and watch that camera's frame-poll cadence (via /api/status or logs) around the fire to confirm the bounded SOAP timeout doesn't visibly delay its next detection pass beyond the timeout window — same acceptance bar already implicitly validated for the existing autotrack inline PTZ call.
8. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` and `cargo test` (new deterrence.rs unit tests + confirm no existing test broke from the AlarmEvent field addition) before any live step; `cd web && npx tsc --noEmit && npm run build` for the frontend.
open_questions: Should the WAV/audio-backchannel half of P2.9 be attempted at all in a near-term follow-up, or should the roadmap entry be re-scoped to "relay/light deterrence" permanently and a genuinely new capability (e.g. a validated go2rtc backchannel-push mechanism) be tracked as its own separate, differently-named feature once real speaker-equipped hardware is available to test against?

The 5 currently-enabled real test cameras (ids 3,4,5,6,8, Dahua/Amcrest per the test-environment memory) may report ZERO ONVIF relay outputs (no alarm-out terminal wired) — if so, the actuation half of this feature can be probe-validated but not physically demonstrated on this hardware; is that an acceptable ship bar, or does it need a cheap USB relay/ONVIF-NVR-with-alarm-out test rig first?

Exact ONVIF DeviceIO XML tag names/casing/namespace prefixes are unverified against real vendor firmware (Dahua vs Amcrest are known to diverge, per onvif_events.rs's own "vendors dialect ONVIF heavily" framing) — the parser must stay best-effort/tolerant; is a wider vendor-compat pass (more test cameras) warranted before calling this "shipped" vs. "shipped, capability-probe-validated on N of M cameras"?

Should the fixed 3-second relay pulse become a per-action configurable duration (would need a new Action field or repurposing `priority`) — recommend hardcoding for v0 and only adding configurability once a real bistable-relay siren box is available to test the UX against.

Should Settings.deterrence_enabled default differently per role (e.g. only an Admin can flip it) even though it rides the generic Operator-writable Settings PUT — given it gates a physical/real-world action, is Operator-level control acceptable or does it warrant joining the Admin-only auth_proxy_*/offsite_* carve-out in auth.rs::min_role_for?

P3.5-openvino-ep-and-clip-zone-state.summary: Two independent sub-features sharing one theme (ONNX-Runtime acceleration surface + CLIP reuse), best shipped as two commits. PART 1 (OpenVINO EP): the codebase already has a fully-formed 'named-accelerator dropdown' per camera (web/src/pages/Cameras.tsx TuneModal, lines 446-458: Inherit global/GPU/CPU) backed by crates/detector/src/lib.rs's build_ort_session() which already does per-OS EP selection (DirectML/CoreML/CUDA) with ort's built-in silent-fallback-to-CPU behavior. Adding OpenVINO is a 4th branch using the exact same mechanics (`ort::execution_providers::OpenVINOExecutionProvider`, confirmed present and unconditionally compiled into ort 2.0.0-rc.10 regardless of cargo feature flags for the is_available() check; the `openvino` cargo feature is only needed to make actual registration succeed). Critical, load-bearing finding from reading ort-sys's build.rs: this repo's pinned `download-binaries` feature fetches prebuilt ONNX Runtime binaries whose feature-set selection logic never branches on 'openvino' — meaning the EP will almost certainly report unavailable out of the box, which is fine and correct given the existing 'gated on availability, never lies about a silently no-op feature' pattern already shipped for CLIP/audio/LPR/face/pose (api.rs capabilities(), Settings.tsx ModelsCard). Backward compatibility drives one key design decision: do NOT change `force_cpu`'s Rust type (bool -> String would hard-break deserializing every existing installation's stored JSON), instead ADD a new `accelerator: String`/`Option<String>` field alongside the untouched `force_cpu`, merged into one dropdown client-side. PART 2 (CLIP zone-state classifier v0): needs zero training and reuses nearly everything — crates/core/src/smart.rs's embed_image()/embed_text()/cosine() (already lazy-cached, already used for P2.2 prompt-based alarm rules and photo-upload search), crates/core/src/pipeline.rs's embed_crop() pixel-bbox-crop-and-embed helper (built for Re-ID), crates/core/src/db.rs's PolyZone zone-scoping pattern, and emit_analytics_event() which already handles event-insert+snapshot+webhook+MQTT+alarm-matching generically for any (label, zone, direction) triple used by crossing/loiter/occupancy/package/residential events today. The one genuinely new piece is a small state-machine module (mirrors crates/core/src/tamper.rs's TamperGate hysteresis exactly) plus periodic per-zone CLIP-crop classification wired into pipeline.rs. A critical placement finding from reading the per-camera loop closely: pipeline.rs line 467 early-`continue`s the rest of frame processing when there's no motion and no tracker/package feature active — so the zone-state block MUST sit above that gate (alongside tamper_detect at lines 310-361), or a static unchanging scene (a closed garage door, the exact case this feature targets) would never get reclassified. v0 emits two fixed, alarm-dropdown-friendly labels ('zone_open'/'zone_closed') scoped per-instance via the existing zone_like substring condition, rather than fully free-text user-typed state names, because Alarms.tsx's object <select> is a closed hardcoded array with no free-text escape hatch today.
already_shipped_adjacent: crates/detector/src/lib.rs build_ort_session() — already selects DirectML/CoreML/CUDA per-OS EP with silent CPU fallback (ort's default `fail_silently()` EP dispatch behavior); OpenVINO is just a 4th branch using the same mechanics.

crates/core/src/smart.rs — embed_image()/embed_text()/cosine() are lazy-loaded, globally-cached CLIP sessions (VISION/TEXT OnceLock<Mutex<..>>) already used for photo-upload search and P2.2 prompt-based alarm rules; zero-shot classification is just cosine(embed(crop), embed(prompt_a)) vs cosine(.., prompt_b) with these exact primitives.
crates/core/src/pipeline.rs embed_crop() (line ~1774) — pixel-bbox crop + CLIP embed helper already built for Re-ID crop embedding; directly reusable for a zone-crop embed.

crates/core/src/pipeline.rs P2.2 fire_prompt_alarms()/prompt_embs cache (line ~1851, ~138) — precedent for caching a CLIP text embedding keyed off a config id, re-embedding only when the prompt text changes.

crates/core/src/tamper.rs TamperGate — precedent for a per-camera hysteresis state machine (enter_frames/clear_frames debounce) that runs on the RAW frame, before privacy masks, and BEFORE the pipeline's motion-based early-continue (pipeline.rs line 467), so it still evaluates on a static/motionless scene — exactly the placement zone-state classification needs.

crates/core/src/pipeline.rs emit_analytics_event() (line 1458) — one call handles event insert (severity auto-computed), snapshot save (honoring no_clip), webhook, MQTT publish, and Alarm Manager matching (zone_ok/confirm_ok) for ANY (label, zone, direction) triple; zero new plumbing needed to make zone-state events alertable.
crates/core/src/db.rs PolyZone (line 58) + GroundCalib (line 149) — established pattern for a zone-scoped, `#[serde(default)]`, no-migration JSON-blob sub-struct.

web/src/pages/Cameras.tsx TuneModal Accelerator <select> (line 446-458) — already a 3-option (inherit/GPU/CPU) named-accelerator dropdown per camera; this is literally the UI the feature spec asks for, just needs a 4th option.

crates/core/src/api.rs capabilities() (line 284) + web/src/pages/Settings.tsx ModelsCard (line 1364) — the exact 'gated on availability, never lies about a feature silently no-op-ing' pattern already used for CLIP/audio/LPR/face/pose model presence; reuse verbatim for an OpenVINO availability row.
web/src/labels.ts prettyLabel() — generic underscore->space fallback already renders any unrecognized label reasonably without a forced edit.
api_endpoints: No new HTTP routes required for either part. Part 1 (accelerator choice) rides the existing PUT /api/settings and PATCH /api/cameras/{id} (detect_config.force_cpu/accelerator) — same routes the existing Accelerator dropdown already uses.
Part 1 UI gating reuses the existing GET /api/capabilities (api.rs capabilities() handler, line 284) — add one more feat(...) entry for OpenVINO EP availability.

Part 2 (zone-state config) rides the existing PATCH /api/cameras/{id} (detect_config.zones[].state_classify) — the same route ZoneEditor's onChange already calls via web/src/api.ts patchCamera() (called from web/src/pages/Cameras.tsx line 173).

Part 2 emitted events ("zone_open"/"zone_closed") are read through the existing GET /api/events, GET /api/events/{id}, and alarm-rule matching — all already RBAC-scoped by allowed_cameras/require_camera; no new read path.

Optional v1 nice-to-have (not required for v0): a POST /api/zones/preview-state debug endpoint to test a zone's two prompts live against the current frame before saving — mirrors the existing 'would have matched' Alarms builder preview pattern (Alarms.tsx matchPreview). Left as an open question / descoped.
db_changes: No SQL migration for either part — everything rides existing JSON-blob columns (Settings row, cameras.detect_json), matching the codebase's `#[serde(default)]` no-migration convention (see db.rs:1003-1005 doc comment on Settings).

db.rs Settings struct (line 1033, next to `pub force_cpu: bool`): add `pub accelerator: String` ("" = auto/per-OS default = current behavior, "cpu", "openvino"). Settings::default() (line 1228-1256): add `accelerator: String::new(),` next to `force_cpu: false,` (line 1256).

db.rs DetectConfig struct (line 208, next to `pub force_cpu: Option<bool>`): add `pub accelerator: Option<String>` (per-camera override; None inherits Settings.accelerator). DetectConfig already `#[derive(Default)]` (line 157-158) so no Default-impl edit needed.
db.rs PolyZone struct (lines 58-102, after the `water` field at line 101): add `pub state_classify: Option<ZoneStateClassify>`.

db.rs new struct (place near GroundCalib, lines 145-155): `ZoneStateClassify { enabled: bool, prompt_open: String, prompt_closed: String, poll_secs: u32 }` with `#[derive(Clone, Debug, Default, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq)] #[serde(default)]` matching GroundCalib's exact shape.

Deliberately NOT touched: `force_cpu: bool`/`Option<bool>` keep their exact current type — a string-typed rename would hard-fail JSON deserialization of every existing installation's already-stored `"force_cpu": true/false` (serde type mismatch is a decode ERROR, not a silent default, even under `#[serde(default)]`, since that attribute only fires when a key is ABSENT). The new `accelerator` field is additive precisely to avoid this backward-compat break.

events table: no new column. Zone-state transitions reuse existing `label` (the fixed strings "zone_open"/"zone_closed"), `zone` (the PolyZone name, for zone_like alarm scoping), `direction` left None — all already existing columns/params on db.add_event_dir (db.rs:1718).
P3.5-openvino-ep-and-clip-zone-state.descope_recommendation: "Split into two independently shippable slices; ship Part 1 (OpenVINO EP) first since it's self-contained S/M work, then Part 2 (zone-state classifier) as its own commit. v0 for Part 2: (a) exactly TWO fixed, hardcoded event labels ('zone_open'/'zone_closed') rather than fully user-typed custom label names — because web/src/pages/Alarms.tsx's object <select> (line 698) is a closed hardcoded LABELS array with no free-text escape hatch, and teaching it to accept arbitrary per-zone custom labels is a real UI-pattern change, not a mechanical one. Users distinguish instances via the existing zone_like substring-match Alarms condition (e.g. label=zone_open + zone_like='Garage'). (b) One classify per zone (a single open/closed binary), not N arbitrary named states — covers garage/gate/pool-cover/mailbox-flag equally well and keeps the state machine trivial. (c) No live prompt-preview endpoint in the ZoneEditor UI (v1 nice-to-have, mirrors the existing Alarms 'would have matched' builder preview) — v0 requires save + wait one poll_secs interval to see it classify. (d) No OpenVINO device_type/precision/num_streams tuning UI (with_device_type/with_num_threads/with_cache_dir etc. on OpenVINOExecutionProvider) — v0 exposes only the on/off EP choice, defaulting to OpenVINO's own device auto-selection. Defer per-device (CPU/GPU/NPU) sub-selection to v1 if real hardware validation shows it's needed. IMPORTANT REALITY CHECK for whoever implements Part 1: `ort`'s `download-binaries` feature (the one this repo uses, detector/Cargo.toml) fetches pyke's prebuilt ONNX Runtime binaries, and their build-feature-selection logic (ort-sys build.rs ~line 528) only branches on cuda/tensorrt/rocm/training/webgpu — NOT openvino — meaning the stock downloaded onnxruntime.dll almost certainly does NOT have the OpenVINO EP compiled in. `is_available()` will honestly return false on a stock install (correctly hiding the dropdown option per the capabilities-gating pattern), and the feature only becomes real if a user manually swaps in an OpenVINO-flavored onnxruntime build (undocumented today). Ship the plumbing + the honest availability gate now; document the manual-swap requirement in README's #optional-ai-models-style section as a v1 follow-up, exactly like the existing 'external binaries are not vendored' convention in CLAUDE.md."

new_files.purpose: Pure, unit-tested helpers for the zone-state classifier, mirroring crates/core/src/tamper.rs's structure: (1) `zone_pixel_bbox(points: &[[f32;2]], fw: f32, fh: f32) -> Option<(u32,u32,u32,u32)>` — min/max over the zone polygon's fraction points scaled to pixels and clamped to the frame, reusing PolyZone.points directly (pure math, fully unit-testable without any model). (2) `classify(cos_open: f32, cos_closed: f32, min_margin: f32) -> Option<bool>` — zero-shot decision between the two cached prompt-embedding cosine scores; None when the margin is too thin (ambiguous frame, don't update state) else Some(is_open). (3) `struct ZoneStateTracker { .. }` with an `observe(&mut self, is_open: bool, confirm_frames: u32) -> Option<bool>` hysteresis method (consecutive-observation debounce before flipping, exactly mirroring TamperGate's enter_frames/clear_frames pattern in tamper.rs) so lighting flicker or a momentary CLIP misfire can't flap the state — returns Some(new_state) only on a confirmed, debounced transition (the edge worth emitting an event for).

open_questions: Should the OpenVINO device_type ('CPU'/'GPU'/'GPU.0'/'NPU'/'HETERO:NPU,GPU') be user-selectable in v0, or hardcoded to OpenVINOExecutionProvider::default() (its own auto-selection)? Recommend deferring to v1 pending real Intel hardware to validate against — this repo's dev/test box has no confirmed Intel iGPU/NPU to validate on.

Confirmed via ort-sys build.rs inspection: the pinned `download-binaries` prebuilt ONNX Runtime almost certainly lacks OpenVINO compiled in (its feature_set selection only branches on cuda/tensorrt/rocm/training/webgpu). Does the team want to (a) ship the honest-but-inert plumbing now and document a manual onnxruntime-swap path, or (b) hold Part 1 until a real OpenVINO-enabled onnxruntime distribution is sourced/vendored? Recommend (a), consistent with the 'external binaries not vendored' convention already in CLAUDE.md.

Free-text custom state labels (e.g. a user typing 'mailbox flag up'/'mailbox flag down' as the literal alarm-matchable label) vs the fixed zone_open/zone_closed pair recommended for v0 — is the fixed pair (scoped per-instance via the existing zone_like substring condition) an acceptable v0 constraint, or does the Alarms.tsx object <select> need to grow a free-text/combobox escape hatch as part of this feature rather than a follow-up?

poll_secs default/floor: CLIP vision inference costs a real ONNX run per zone per poll (~tens of ms on CPU, since embed_image is CPU-pinned per smart.rs's design comment). What's an acceptable default cadence (proposed: 15s) and should there be a hard minimum (e.g. 5s) enforced server-side to bound cost on a camera with several classify-zones configured?

Should zone-state classification respect `cam.detect_config.event_only_recording` / retention interactions the way other analytics events do, or is there any reason it should be exempt? (Recommend: no special-case — it already rides emit_analytics_event identically to crossing/loiter/package, so it inherits standard retention/flagging behavior for free.)

P3.5-openvino-ep-and-clip-zone-state.validation_plan: "Part 1 (OpenVINO EP), against the running release NVR on :8080: (1) Before code changes, run `cargo tree -p zoomy | grep -i openvino` to confirm baseline absence, then after adding the Cargo.toml feature, rebuild release and grep the binary/build log for onnxruntime provider list. (2) Hit GET /api/capabilities and confirm a new 'openvino' row appears with `present` reflecting the real is_available() result on this dev box (expected: false, since this is an AMD/NVIDIA-class dev machine per CLAUDE.md's DirectML/CUDA validation history, not an Intel box) — this IS the correct, honest result and proves the gating works rather than lying. (3) Force-select 'openvino' via a direct PATCH /api/cameras/{id} with detect_config.accelerator='openvino' on one of the 5 enabled test cameras (3,4,5,6,8) and confirm in the server log that registration falls back to CPU with a warn-level 'OpenVINO not available at runtime; falling back to CPU' line (mirrors the existing log_ep() pattern) rather than crashing or silently producing zero detections — detection events must keep flowing normally on that camera. (4) Confirm the existing GPU/CPU dropdown paths (DirectML on this Windows box) are completely unaffected — a regression here would be a live detection outage across all 5 enabled cameras. Part 2 (CLIP zone-state), against the same NVR: (1) Confirm crate::smart::models_present() is true on this box (CLIP models already validated live per CLAUDE.md's P2.2/Re-ID history) — if not, download-guide-gate first. (2) On one static-scene camera, draw a small PolyZone over a stable object/area, enable state_classify with two deliberately distinguishable prompts (e.g. 'an empty driveway' vs 'a car parked in the driveway'), set poll_secs low (e.g. 5) for fast validation. (3) Watch the pipeline log for the periodic classify tick firing even with ZERO motion in the scene (proves the placement-before-the-motion-continue fix works) — pull a car into/out of the zone between polls and confirm exactly one zone_open/zone_closed event fires per real transition (not per poll) via GET /api/events?camera_id=N&label=zone_open. (4) Create an Alarms rule with label=zone_open + zone_like=<the zone name>, confirm the 'would have matched' builder preview (Alarms.tsx matchPreview) shows candidates, then trigger a real transition and confirm the configured webhook/ntfy action actually fires. (5) Confirm a privacy-masked overlap case: put a privacy mask over the classify zone, verify classification still runs (uses the raw pre-mask frame) but the SAVED event snapshot is correctly redacted (mirrors the tamper dual-path validation already done for #63)."

P3.9.descope_recommendation: "Ship in two slices even within this already-STOPped scope. v0 = segments-only pull ('pull-based offsite-in-reverse'): the archive_pull worker discovers selected remote cameras via GET /api/cameras, auto-creates local disabled/no-detect/no-record placeholder cameras (add_camera+update_camera, sanitized name/group per restore()'s valid_name/valid_group/no_control checks), and syncs ONLY segments via the new since-ascending /api/recordings + GET /api/recordings/{id}/video, writing into recordings_dir/<placeholder>/ and calling the existing upsert_segment — this needs ZERO new tables (upsert_segment's UNIQUE(path) already gives idempotency) and gets retention pruning for free from record.rs's existing per-camera/global-byte-cap pass. This alone delivers the core disaster-recovery value (durable off-box video copies, browsable in the existing Recordings/Timeline UI) with the smallest possible surface: 1 new db fn, 1 query-param extension, 1 worker file, 1 Settings card. v1 = layer in events (GET /api/events ascending + GET /api/snapshots/{file}), which is what actually needs the new pulled_events idempotency table and the richer insert helper, since without it there's no natural unique key to dedupe events across ticks/restarts. Recommend shipping v0 first, live-validating the two-box round-trip end-to-end, THEN adding events as a fast-follow commit rather than one large PR — matches this repo's established 'one validated commit at a time' working style."]
P3.9.security_rbac_notes: "Real gap to document, not silently paper over: a Bearer API token today has user_id=None, so allowed_cameras() (api.rs 1149-1165) returns None = UNRESTRICTED for every camera regardless of the token's role — there is no per-camera token scoping (unlike named users via user_cameras/#66). This means the 'selected cameras' the feature spec asks for can only be enforced CLIENT-SIDE (the pull worker simply never requests non-selected cameras); the minted token itself grants read access to ALL of the primary's cameras/segments/events/snapshots to anyone who obtains it. Mitigate by: (1) recommending/enforcing role=viewer for pull tokens (least privilege — GET-only per min_role_for, api.rs default Method::GET => Role::Viewer), (2) documenting in the Settings card copy that the token can read every camera on the primary, (3) telling the admin to mint a DEDICATED token for this purpose (POST /api/tokens) so it can be independently revoked (DELETE /api/tokens/{id}, already shipped) if the secondary box is ever compromised, without affecting other integrations. Also required: put_settings must Admin-gate any delta to the new archive_pull_* fields exactly like the existing auth_proxy_*/offsite_* block (api.rs 5404-5426) since the field holds a live credential and determines what remote host this box trusts and pulls arbitrary video/image bytes from; archive_pull_token must be write-only (blank-on-GET, blank-preserves-on-PUT) exactly like offsite_secret_key/smtp_pass. The endpoint URL must be validated the same way offsite_endpoint is (http(s):// scheme required, no_control chars, per put_settings 5453-5467) before the worker ever calls ureq against it — a raw admin-entered string reaching an outbound HTTP client. Remote-supplied data used to build LOCAL state must be sanitized through the same gates add_camera/restore already use (valid_name for the placeholder camera name — lowercase/digits/_/- only, <=32 chars per api.rs valid_name 1344-1350 — free-text remote camera names will usually need slugifying; valid_group for the human-readable label; no_control on anything interpolated). Bound the worker like offsite.rs bounds itself: a MAX_SEGMENT_BYTES-style cap on a single downloaded segment/snapshot so a misbehaving or malicious 'primary' can't OOM or fill the secondary's disk, and a MAX_PER_TICK cap so a huge backlog can't starve the secondary's own local detection/recording threads."]
P3.9.validation_plan: "Needs a genuine two-box setup since P3.9 is inherently cross-instance; the running :8080 NVR (7 cameras) becomes the PRIMARY and a second instance becomes the SECONDARY: (1) On :8080, POST /api/tokens {name:'pull-secondary', role:'viewer'} and capture the raw zoomy_<hex> token (shown once); confirm GET /api/audit shows token_created. (2) Start a second zoomy instance on a separate --data-dir and --port 8081 (per the memory GOTCHA: use a detached PowerShell Start-Process, NOT a timed background shell — the data-dir lock is per-directory so a distinct dir avoids the exclusivity lock entirely) with no real cameras configured. (3) On :8081's Settings, enable Pull archive with endpoint http://127.0.0.1:8080, paste the token, select exactly one real camera name from :8080 to keep the first run fast. (4) Poll GET /api/archive/status on :8081 until it reports a non-zero segment/event count and a recent last_ok_ts. (5) GET /api/cameras on :8081 and confirm exactly one new camera appeared, enabled=false, correctly named/grouped. (6) GET /api/recordings?camera_id=<that id> on :8081 lists pulled SegmentRows; GET /api/recordings/{id}/video returns a playable MP4 (curl -I for 200 + content-type, then actually open it in Chrome or VLC to confirm it plays, not just that bytes arrived). (7) Open the :8081 web UI (Chrome) on Recordings/Events for that camera and confirm playback works through the UNMODIFIED existing Timeline/CrossTimeline/event-viewer components — this is the key proof that the reuse strategy actually works end-to-end, not just at the API layer. (8) Stop and restart the :8081 instance (detached Start-Process again), confirm the sync resumes without re-downloading already-pulled segments (segment count unchanged, disk bytes unchanged) and without duplicate events (query the secondary's own zoomy.db events table row count for that camera before/after via sqlite3 CLI). (9) Negative test: set an invalid/revoked token (DELETE /api/tokens/{id} on :8080 first) and confirm /api/archive/status on :8081 surfaces an auth failure honestly rather than crashing the worker thread or corrupting the cursor table. (10) Confirm the pulled camera never appears on :8081's Live grid (enabled=false) and that :8081's own retention pass (wait past RETENTION_EVERY or check logs) applies to the pulled segments using that placeholder camera's retention_days — verifiable by setting a very short per-camera retention override in the Cameras tuning modal on :8081 and watching a pulled segment get pruned on the next cycle."]

new_files.purpose: The pull-client worker: struct Target::from_settings(&Settings) (validated endpoint parsing, mirrors offsite.rs's Target, ~50 lines); run(db, shutdown) tick loop (mirrors offsite::run, TICK ~60s); run_once() that: (1) GETs {endpoint}/api/cameras with Bearer auth to resolve selected remote camera names -> remote ids + ensures a local disabled/no-detect/no-record placeholder Camera exists per selected name (add_camera+update_camera, sanitized via shared valid_name/valid_group/no_control-style checks — likely reusing api.rs's validators via a pub(crate) fn or duplicating the small pure checks to avoid a cross-module dependency on api.rs handlers); (2) for each selected camera, reads its archive_pull_cursor row, calls GET /api/recordings?camera_id=X&since=T (ASC), downloads each segment's bytes via GET /api/recordings/{id}/video (bounded like offsite's MAX_SEGMENT_BYTES), writes to recordings_dir/<placeholder-name>/<remote-id-or-start_ts>.mp4, calls db.upsert_segment (idempotent on path); (3) calls GET /api/events?camera_id=X&since_asc=T (ASC), downloads each event's snapshot via GET /api/snapshots/{file}, calls db.insert_pulled_event (idempotent via pulled_events); (4) advances/persists the cursor, bounded MAX_PER_TICK like offsite; error/backoff handling and a stats/status readout function for the new GET /api/archive/status handler, all closely mirroring offsite.rs's structure end to end.

open_questions: Should the very first sync of a newly-configured remote camera backfill ALL retained history on the primary (cursor starts at 0/epoch), or default to 'from now on' (cursor starts at time-of-configuration) to avoid a large surprise initial transfer? Recommend defaulting to 'from now' with an explicit opt-in 'backfill existing history' action, since a full historical pull of a multi-day retention window could be a large one-time transfer the admin should consciously trigger.

What happens if a selected remote camera is later RENAMED on the primary? v0's cursor/placeholder-camera keying is by name (single-remote assumption per the descope) — a rename would be seen as a brand-new remote camera, creating a second local placeholder rather than continuing the same one. Worth documenting as a known v0 limitation rather than solving (would need a stable remote camera id + endpoint composite key, which starts to look like real federation).

Where exactly should the placeholder camera's video files live on disk — the shared recordings_dir/<name>/ (gets free retention reuse but competes for the pooled global retention_gb byte cap against the secondary's own live cameras, if it has any) vs. a dedicated directory outside the pooled cap (clean isolation but needs hand-rolled retention code, forfeiting the free-retention-reuse win this plan is built around)? This report recommends the shared-dir approach and documents the byte-cap tradeoff, but it's a real product decision worth confirming before implementation.

Does a 'secondary' box in practice also run its own live cameras/pipeline (dual-purpose NVR + archive puller), or is it expected to be an otherwise-empty appliance dedicated purely to archiving? This affects how aggressively MAX_PER_TICK/bandwidth should be throttled to avoid starving co-located live detection.

Should the primary-side GET /api/archive/status-equivalent (i.e., does the PRIMARY need any awareness that it's being pulled from, e.g. a small 'connected archivers' list for the admin's peace of mind) be in scope, or is the primary intentionally unaware (it's just an ordinary Bearer-token API consumer, per the 'reuse existing endpoints, no new federation concept' instruction)? This report assumes the primary needs zero new code beyond the two ascending-query additions (which are generically useful anyway, not archive-specific) and the token's normal last_used_ts/audit trail is sufficient visibility.
P3.9.summary: P3.9 pulls selected cameras' segments+events from a primary Cammy instance into a secondary one over the existing HTTP API, authenticated with the existing api_tokens Bearer mechanism (matrix #48, unchanged). The design maximizes reuse: the primary needs only two additive, backward-compatible extensions — an ascending `since` query on GET /api/recordings (new db::list_segments_since) and an ascending `since_asc` query on GET /api/events (new db::list_events_since) — plus the ALREADY-EXISTING GET /api/recordings/{id}/video, GET /api/snapshots/{file}, and GET /api/cameras are reused verbatim as the actual data-fetch endpoints. The secondary runs a new background worker (crates/core/src/archive_pull.rs), structurally cloned from offsite.rs's Target/run/run_once/backoff pattern (the S3-backup worker, just pull instead of push): it discovers selected remote camera names via GET /api/cameras, auto-creates a local disabled/no-detect/no-record placeholder Camera per one (reusing restore()'s add_camera+update_camera by-name pattern), downloads segment bytes into the standard recordings_dir/<name>/ layout and registers them via the existing idempotent upsert_segment (free retention reuse from record.rs's existing per-camera/global retention pass — a major win, zero new retention code), and downloads event snapshots + metadata into the normal events table via a new insert_pulled_event guarded by a new small pulled_events idempotency table (keeps the high-traffic events table schema untouched). Because pulled footage lands in the ordinary cameras/segments/events tables, the ENTIRE existing Events/Recordings/Timeline/CrameraDetail web UI renders and plays it with zero new frontend beyond one new Settings card (mirrors the Offsite backup card almost verbatim) and one new status endpoint. The one real gap surfaced by reading auth.rs: Bearer tokens have no per-camera RBAC scoping (allowed_cameras returns unrestricted for any token), so 'selected cameras' can only be enforced client-side in v0 — documented prominently as a security note rather than silently assumed away.

already_shipped_adjacent: crates/core/src/record.rs record::run's SEGMENT_QUIET_SECS/index/retention loop (lines 23-270) — the exact pattern (scan dir -> db.upsert_segment -> age+byte-cap prune) the import job's segment bookkeeping mirrors

crates/core/src/pipeline.rs save_snapshot (line 2014), zone_for (line 1810), passes_zones_and_size (line 1714) — the exact per-detection filtering/annotation logic the live pipeline uses, reused verbatim by the import worker

crates/core/src/db.rs Db::upsert_segment (line 3402), Db::add_event/add_event_dir (lines 1696-1744), Db::camera_by_name (line 3071), Db::prune_events_before's flagged-exemption (line 3793) — all reused as-is, no schema change
crates/core/src/go2rtc.rs desired_streams (line 38) already filters on cam.enabled, so an enabled=false camera is automatically invisible to go2rtc/live streaming with zero new code

crates/core/src/record.rs desired-recording filter (line 61-72, enabled && record) and health.rs (line 30, !cam.enabled -> continue) — both already exclude a disabled camera from live recording attempts and false-offline health alerts

crates/core/src/db.rs Camera.detect_config.retention_days per-camera override (line 232-238, '0 = keep indefinitely') and events.flagged retention exemption (#47) — both existing mechanisms the import job leans on to protect historical footage/events from the global retention sweep

api_endpoints: POST /api/import — body {name, path, started_at?, detect?}; creates (or reuses an existing virtual camera by that name) a Camera row with source="virtual:<slug>", enabled=false, record=true, detect_config.retention_days=Some(0); rejects if the name already belongs to a real (non-virtual) camera or an import is already running; kicks off the background remux+detect job via tokio::task::spawn_blocking into the new import::run_import; returns {camera_id, status:"queued"}. RBAC: default Operator (mutation).

GET /api/import/{camera_id} — polls crate::import::ImportBoard for {status: queued|remuxing|detecting|done|error, segments_written, frames_processed, events_found, error}; require_camera-scoped (404 if the caller can't see this camera, consistent with every other single-camera GET). RBAC: default Viewer (read).
db_changes: NONE to the cameras table schema — deliberately reuses Camera.source's existing scheme-prefix convention ("virtual:<slug>", parallel to exec:/ffmpeg:/onvif:) as the virtual-camera marker instead of a new column, and Camera.enabled=false (already fully gates a camera out of go2rtc/record.rs/pipeline.rs with zero code changes to those three files).
NONE to events/segments schema — reuses the existing detect_config.retention_days JSON-blob field (per-camera override, no migration) and the existing events.flagged column.

One NEW query method (not a schema change): Db::flag_events_in_range(camera_id, from_ts, to_ts) -> Result<usize> in db.rs, appended near prune_events_before (~line 3793-3809): 'UPDATE events SET flagged=1 WHERE camera_id=?1 AND ts BETWEEN ?2 AND ?3'. Needed because prune_events_before deletes any non-flagged event older than the GLOBAL event_retention_days (default 30 — db.rs line 1251) regardless of camera; without this, importing footage older than 30 days would have its events deleted on the very next 60s retention tick in record.rs (which this feature does NOT edit).
P3.10.descope_recommendation: v0 (this plan): path-import only — the caller supplies a path already reachable on the server's local filesystem (natural for the desktop app / NAS deployments); no browser multipart upload (large video files over HTTP need DefaultBodyLimit raised + streaming-to-disk, a materially bigger and riskier slice). Detection pass is plain YOLO only at a fixed ~1 sampled frame/sec, applying the camera's labels/min_score/zones (reusing passes_zones_and_size/zone_for) — NO motion gating, face recognition, LPR, audio/transcription, tracker/analytics (crossing/loiter/occupancy), or CLIP embeddings for imported footage; these are the same per-frame cost centers the live pipeline pays and are straightforward v1 additions once the plumbing exists. Imported events do NOT fire alarm rules, webhooks, ntfy, email, or MQTT (deliberate — nobody wants a phone buzz for a car that already happened last week); they're written straight to the events table like the live pipeline does, just without the notify::fire/mqtt_tx/genai_tx calls. Exactly one import job runs system-wide at a time (simple in-process guard, no queue); a second POST while one is in flight gets 409. No cancel button — abandoning an in-flight import means deleting the camera once it's done. No motion_grid population, so retroactive region motion search (P2.3) doesn't cover imported footage. Segment length uses the same Settings.segment_seconds as live recording; frame-sample rate and a max-import-duration guard are hardcoded, not user-configurable. v1 candidates (not in this plan): multipart upload, face/plate/audio parity, alarm-rule opt-in ("notify as if live"), job cancel + a real progress percentage, ZoneEditor using a segment thumbnail instead of the (unreachable) live frame.jpg so a virtual camera's zones/masks can be tuned visually.

files_to_edit.why: AppState struct (lines 20-50) gains an `import_board: crate::import::ImportBoard` field (same pattern as `status: StatusBoard` line 31 / `onvif_inspector` line 49). router() (lines 52-184) gains two routes: `POST /api/import` and `GET /api/import/{camera_id}`, placed near the existing `/api/cameras/{id}/timelapse` block (lines 70-71). Two new handler fns (e.g. near create_timelapse, ~line 3135): `start_import` (validates name via the existing `valid_name` at line 1344-1350, looks up/creates the virtual camera via `db.camera_by_name`(3071)+`db.add_camera`(1637)+`db.update_camera`(1667), forces enabled=false + detect_config.retention_days=Some(0), then `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` into `import::run_import` — mirrors create_timelapse's background-job dispatch at lines 3008-3135 and search_by_image's spawn_blocking-for-CPU-work at lines 3917-3925) and `import_status` (reads `st.import_board`, RBAC via `allowed_cameras`/`require_camera` at lines 1149-1186, same as every other single-camera GET e.g. recording_at at 4198).

Add `mod import;` to the module list (lines 12-49, alphabetically near `mod health;`). Add `import_board: crate::import::ImportBoard::default()` to the `AppState { ... }` literal (lines 332-349) alongside the other worker-shared handles.

Bump 3 helper fns from module-private `fn` to `pub(crate) fn` so the new import module (same crate, different module) can reuse them verbatim: `save_snapshot` (line 2014, draws motion/detection boxes and writes the event JPEG), `zone_for` (line 1810, resolves a detection's Required-zone name), `passes_zones_and_size` (line 1714, applies min_area/max_area + Required/Ignore zone filtering). Pure visibility-keyword edits, zero logic change — low actual conflict risk despite pipeline.rs being a hot file, since nothing else in the function bodies moves.

Bump `SEGMENT_PATTERN` (line 19, `const SEGMENT_PATTERN: &str = "%Y%m%d-%H%M%S.mp4"`) to `pub const`, so the import job names its remuxed segment files with the exact same strftime convention `recorder::scan_segments`/`recorder::prune` expect (retention's age-cutoff pass re-derives start_ts by parsing the FILENAME, not the DB row — the filename must encode the true historical timestamp, not import wall-clock time).

Append one new method near `prune_events_before` (line 3793-3809): `pub fn flag_events_in_range(&self, camera_id: i64, from_ts: i64, to_ts: i64) -> Result<usize>` — bulk-sets `flagged=1` on every event the import job just created, so they're exempt from the global `event_retention_days` prune (same exemption `prune_events_before` already honors for flagged rows) regardless of how old the imported footage's timestamps are.

Add an `ImportProgress` interface and two client fns (`startImport`, `importStatus`) near the `timelapse:` poll-pattern client fn (line 700-704, which is the closest existing analog: POST kicks a background job, GET-style poll returns {status, ...}).

Add a new `details.adv` "Import footage" section, mirroring the "Add a camera" section's structure exactly (lines 1093-1201: form fields, `busy` state, `onChange()` refresh-on-success) — fields: name, server file path, "footage began at" datetime (default now), detect toggle; submit calls `api.startImport`, then polls `api.importStatus(camera_id)` on an interval (same idea as Recordings.tsx's timelapse poll) until done/error. Also tweak the per-row status cell (lines 1022-1030, currently renders "disabled" for any `!cam.enabled` row) to special-case `cam.source.startsWith("virtual:")` and show an "imported" badge instead, so a virtual camera doesn't read as a fault/off-camera.

new_files.purpose: Virtual-camera footage import worker. Holds `ImportStatus`/`ImportProgress` (Serialize) and `ImportBoard` (Arc<RwLock<HashMap<camera_id, ImportProgress>>>, same shape as status.rs's StatusBoard) plus a module-level single-job guard (AtomicBool or a 1-permit semaphore, mirroring api.rs's THUMB_SEM pattern). The blocking entry point `pub fn run_import(db: Db, cam: Camera, ffmpeg_bin: Option<PathBuf>, recordings_dir: PathBuf, snapshots_dir: PathBuf, source_path: PathBuf, started_at: i64, board: ImportBoard)`: (1) remux+segment the input file with ffmpeg's `-f segment` muxer (same flags as recorder::Recording::start's codec_args, fed `-i source_path` instead of an RTSP url, no `-strftime` — sequential temp names, then renamed to `recorder::SEGMENT_PATTERN` filenames offset from `started_at` by `i*segment_seconds`), calling `db.upsert_segment` per part (updating `board` as it goes); (2) for each segment, sample frames at ~1 fps via ffmpeg image2 piped JPEGs, decode with `image::load_from_memory`, run a locally-constructed `detector::Detector` (per the camera's `detect_config.model`/`force_cpu` or Settings fallback, same resolution logic as pipeline.rs lines 268-290), filter through `pipeline::passes_zones_and_size` + labels/min_score (mirrors pipeline.rs's `wanted` filter chain, lines 499-510, minus cooldown/tracker), draw+write the snapshot via `pipeline::save_snapshot`, and `db.add_event(...)` with ts = segment start_ts + frame offset — deliberately NOT dispatching notify::fire/mqtt_tx/genai_tx (imported historical footage must not fire live alerts); (3) once all events are written, call `db.flag_events_in_range(cam.id, started_at, started_at+duration)` to protect them from event-retention pruning; updates `board`'s status to Done/Error throughout, with the error message surfaced to the poll endpoint.

open_questions: Is server-local path-import (vs. a browser multipart upload) acceptable for v0? It's natural for the desktop app / NAS deployments (drop the SD card / mounted share, type the path) but doesn't let a pure remote-web user upload straight from their phone.

Auto-flagging every imported event (to survive the global event_retention_days) pre-stars them all in the Events "Saved" filter — is that acceptable UX, or should retention protection instead be a one-time UI warning telling the user to raise event_retention_days first, leaving events unflagged and pruneable?

Should imported segments be fully exempt from retention (including the global byte-cap pass), or is inheriting the same machinery as live recordings (only the per-camera AGE cutoff overridden to 0) the intended behavior? Current design: byte cap still applies.

Is a hardcoded ~1 fps detection sample rate acceptable for v0, or does a long import (e.g. a multi-hour dashcam trip) need a coarser default / a max-duration guard to bound CPU/GPU time and temp-JPEG count?

Db::delete_camera (db.rs) only removes DB rows via FK cascade — it does NOT delete the actual recording/snapshot files on disk. That's a pre-existing gap for every camera, but import users are likely to create/delete many one-off "import" cameras and will notice orphaned files sooner. Worth fixing alongside this feature, or tracked separately?

Is a single system-wide concurrent-import limit (reject a 2nd POST /api/import with 409 while one is running) acceptable, or should it be a small bounded pool (like THUMB_SEM's 3) for multi-core boxes importing several clips at once?
P3.10.security_rbac_notes: Reuses existing per-camera RBAC exactly as every other camera-scoped endpoint does: allowed_cameras/require_camera (api.rs lines 1149-1186) gate GET /api/import/{camera_id} to callers who can see that camera (404, not 403 — no camera-id enumeration, consistent with recording_at/segment_video etc). No auth.rs changes needed — the default method-based rule in min_role_for (line 172-175: GET→Viewer, else→Operator) already gives the right gating for both new routes without a special-case entry (unlike /api/backup, this endpoint never touches camera credentials, so it correctly stays Operator not Admin). The one new trust consideration: POST /api/import reads an arbitrary path on the server's local filesystem and passes it to `ffmpeg -i <path>` via Rust's Command::arg (no shell involved, so no shell-injection vector, unlike go2rtc's YAML string-interpolation which the codebase already hardens against control chars). This is a new local-file-read surface reachable by any Operator — but NOT a new class of escalation for this codebase: an Operator can already achieve arbitrary command execution today via a camera's exec: source (POST /api/cameras is already Operator-gated, and go2rtc.rs's api_addable/config-write path documents exec:/ffmpeg: as accepted, RCE-capable sources). Still validate the path exists and is a regular file up front (clean 400 instead of a cryptic ffmpeg spawn failure), and consider a v1 hardening of restricting imports to a configured root directory if this NVR is ever exposed to lower-trust Operators. Deliberately, imported events never reach notify::fire/mqtt_tx/db.add_notification/genai_tx — historical footage must not trigger live phone pushes, HA/MQTT automations, or webhook side effects for something that already happened.
P3.10.summary: Reuse-heavy design that needs ZERO database schema changes and ZERO edits to go2rtc.rs/record.rs/pipeline.rs's live detection loop. A "virtual camera" is just a normal Camera row (Camera struct/db.rs unchanged) with source="virtual:<slug>" (a new scheme-prefix convention parallel to the existing exec:/ffmpeg:/onvif: ones) and enabled=false, record=true — enabled=false already, with zero code changes, makes go2rtc.rs's desired_streams (line 38-47), record.rs's live-recording desired filter (line 61-72), pipeline.rs's live detect loop (line 251), health.rs (line 30), and mqtt.rs (line 196) all skip it automatically, exactly like any deliberately-paused camera today. A new POST /api/import creates/reuses that camera row, forces its detect_config.retention_days=Some(0) (the existing per-camera "keep indefinitely" override, #67-era, JSON-blob, no migration) so historical footage doesn't get instantly aged out, and dispatches a new crates/core/src/import.rs blocking worker via tokio::task::spawn_blocking. That worker (1) ffmpeg-segments the input file into the exact recordings/{camera}/%Y%m%d-%H%M%S.mp4 layout live recording uses (bumping recorder::SEGMENT_PATTERN to pub, one line) with filenames offset from a caller-supplied historical start time, indexing each part via the existing Db::upsert_segment — so the file is instantly playable through the UNCHANGED Recordings page/segment_video handler; and (2) samples frames at ~1fps and runs the existing detector::Detector against them, reusing pipeline.rs's save_snapshot/zone_for/passes_zones_and_size (bumped from private fn to pub(crate) fn, pure visibility change) to write real, zone/label/min-score-filtered events via the existing Db::add_event — deliberately WITHOUT dispatching alarms/webhook/MQTT/push (imported history must not trigger live notifications). A new Db::flag_events_in_range bulk-sets events.flagged=1 (existing column) on the imported range so the global event_retention_days sweep in record.rs (unedited) can't silently delete them. GET /api/import/{camera_id} polls a small new in-memory ImportBoard (same Arc<RwLock<HashMap>> shape as status.rs's StatusBoard) for progress. Web side adds an "Import footage" details.adv section to Cameras.tsx next to the existing "Add a camera" form (same component, same onChange-refresh wiring), and a one-line tweak so a virtual camera's status cell reads "imported" instead of "disabled".
